chapters.title: SOVEREIGNTY OF THE PEOPLE AND SUPREMACY OF THIS CONSTITUTION
chapters.fullTitle: Chapter ONE – SOVEREIGNTY OF THE PEOPLE AND SUPREMACY OF THIS CONSTITUTION

sections.title: Sovereignty of the people
sections.simplified: All power in Kenya ultimately belongs to you — the ordinary citizen. Politicians, judges, and government offices only hold power because you gave it to them through this Constitution. If they misuse it, they are acting against you.
examples: Kamau votes in the general election and chooses his MP. That MP now holds power on Kamau's behalf — not their own power, but power borrowed from the people.

A government official tries to shut down a public protest without legal authority. Under this article, they have no right to do that — the people are the source of all power, and silencing them without cause goes against the Constitution.

sections.title: Supremacy of this Constitution
sections.simplified: The Constitution is the highest law in Kenya — above Parliament, above the President, above any court ruling or government policy. If any law or government action clashes with the Constitution, the Constitution wins and that law is thrown out.
examples: Parliament passes a law allowing police to arrest people without any reason. Because the Constitution protects freedom from arbitrary arrest, that law is automatically void — it has no force no matter who signed it.
A county governor issues a directive that denies women from inheriting land in her county. That directive is unconstitutional and has no legal standing — the Constitution overrides it.

sections.title: Defence of this Constitution
sections.simplified: Defending the Constitution is not just a job for soldiers or judges — it is every Kenyan's duty. If anyone, including the military or a political group, tries to take over the government outside the rules set by the Constitution, that is illegal and must be resisted.
examples: A group of senior military officers announces they are dissolving Parliament and taking control of government. Every Kenyan — civilians, civil servants, judges — has a duty to refuse to recognise that government because it violates this article.

Wanjiku learns that a county official is systematically ignoring court orders and running the county as if laws do not apply to them. She has the right — and the duty — to report this and take legal action to defend constitutional order.

chapters.title: THE REPUBLIC
chapters.description: What defines Kenya as a state

sections.title: Declaration of the Republic
sections.simplified: Kenya is an independent, self-governing country that belongs to no other nation. Its government must be democratic — meaning multiple political parties can compete for power, and citizens choose their leaders through free elections.
examples: A foreign government tries to pressure Kenya's President to ban opposition parties ahead of an election. The Constitution makes that impossible — Kenya is a multi-party democracy and no outside power can change that.
Hassan wants to register a new political party to challenge the ruling government. Under this article, he has the right to do so — Kenya's democratic foundation protects political competition.

sections.title: Territory of Kenya
sections.simplified: Kenya's borders — on land and at sea — are fixed and legally protected. The country cannot lose territory or gain new territory unless Parliament passes a law specifically allowing it.
examples: A neighbouring country begins erecting structures on land that has historically been part of Kenya. The Kenyan government is constitutionally required to defend that territory as part of Kenya's sovereign land.

A private company claims ownership of an offshore ocean area and tries to exclude Kenyan fishermen. That area falls within Kenya's territorial waters — it belongs to Kenya and the fishermen have every right to be there.

sections.title: Devolution and access to services
sections.simplified: Kenya runs on two levels of government — national and county — and both must work together. National government services must be reachable from anywhere in the country, not just from Nairobi.
examples: Residents of a remote area in Turkana struggle to access national ID registration services because the government has no office in their area. Under this article, the national government must ensure those services reach them — either through mobile offices or other means.

The national government tries to bypass the county government and directly implement a project in Kisumu without consultation. The Constitution requires both levels to cooperate — neither can simply ignore the other.

sections.title: National, official and other languages
sections.simplified: Kiswahili is Kenya's national identity language. Both Kiswahili and English are official — so government business can be done in either. The government must also actively support local Kenyan languages and make communication accessible to people who are deaf or have other disabilities.
examples: A deaf Kenyan goes to court and finds there is no sign language interpreter available. The Constitution requires the State to promote Kenyan Sign Language as a means of communication — the court should provide one.

A community in the Coast region wants their local language taught in primary school. The Constitution obligates the State to promote indigenous languages, so they have a strong constitutional basis to petition the government for this.

sections.title: State and religion
sections.simplified: Kenya does not have an official religion. The government cannot favour Christianity, Islam, or any other faith over another. Every Kenyan is free to practise their religion without the government pushing a particular one.
examples: A county government decides to only fund churches in its area and refuses to allocate any resources to mosques or Hindu temples. This violates the Constitution — the State must remain neutral on religion.

A school principal insists that all students attend a Christian morning assembly as a government requirement. That crosses the line — the State has no religion, and forcing children into religious observance is unconstitutional.

sections.title: National symbols and national days
sections.simplified: Kenya's flag, anthem, coat of arms, and public seal are protected national symbols representing all Kenyans. The three national holidays — Madaraka, Mashujaa, and Jamhuri Day — are guaranteed public holidays that every employed Kenyan is entitled to.
examples: An employer tells his workers they must come in on Jamhuri Day and will not be paid extra. That is illegal — Jamhuri Day is a constitutionally protected public holiday and workers are entitled to it.

A business prints commercial packaging that uses the national coat of arms to imply government endorsement. The national symbols have specific constitutional status and cannot be used commercially in a misleading way.

sections.title: National values and principles of governance
sections.simplified: Every government official, every public officer, and every State institution must act with integrity, treat people equally, be transparent, and uphold human dignity. These are not suggestions — they are binding obligations on anyone exercising public power.
examples: A county health officer is caught awarding medical supply contracts to her relatives without a competitive process. She is violating the national values of integrity, accountability, and transparency — which are binding on her as a public officer.

A government department consistently ignores complaints from a minority community when allocating bursaries. This violates the national values of non-discrimination, inclusiveness, and equity — the community can legally challenge this in court.

sections.title: Culture
sections.simplified: Kenya's diverse cultures are constitutionally recognised and protected. If a company or institution uses your community's cultural practices, songs, designs, or indigenous plants for profit, your community is entitled to compensation. The government must also actively support arts and cultural expression.
examples: A foreign fashion brand uses traditional Maasai beadwork patterns in their clothing line without permission or payment to the Maasai community. The Constitution requires that communities receive compensation for the use of their cultural heritage — the community can legally demand payment.

A seed company patents a variety of traditional sorghum that a community in Baringo has grown for generations. The Constitution specifically protects indigenous seeds and plant varieties — that community's ownership must be recognised.

chapters.title: CITIZENSHIP
chapters.description: Who is Kenyan and what that means

sections.title: Entitlements of citizens
sections.simplified: Being a Kenyan citizen comes with concrete entitlements — including your right to a passport. The government cannot refuse to give you a passport, or take it away, without following a specific law that meets constitutional standards. No official can just decide to confiscate your travel documents on a whim.
examples: Atieno applies for her first Kenyan passport but is told by a government clerk that she must pay an unofficial fee or it will be denied. That is illegal — she is entitled to her passport as a citizen, and the clerk cannot arbitrarily block it.

A government official threatens to cancel a politician's passport to prevent him from travelling abroad. Confiscating a passport can only be done through a proper legal process under a specific Act of Parliament — not by an official's personal decision.

sections.title: Retention and acquisition of citizenship
sections.simplified: Your Kenyan citizenship cannot be taken away just because you got married to a foreigner or divorced. Marriage does not make you less Kenyan, and divorce does not strip you of citizenship you were born with or legally acquired.
examples: Chebet, a Kenyan woman, marries a British man and moves to the UK. Some relatives claim she is no longer Kenyan. They are wrong — the Constitution is clear that marriage does not end Kenyan citizenship.

After a divorce, Wanjiku's ex-husband claims she lost her Kenyan citizenship when she registered as a permanent resident in South Africa during their marriage. That is false — citizenship acquired by birth is not lost through marriage or its dissolution.

sections.title: Citizenship by birth
sections.simplified: You are automatically Kenyan from birth if either of your parents is or was Kenyan — it does not matter where in the world you were born. Even a child found abandoned in Kenya with no known parents is treated as a Kenyan citizen by the Constitution.
examples: Amina is born in Qatar to a Kenyan father and a Qatari mother. Even though she was born abroad and her mother is not Kenyan, Amina is a Kenyan citizen by birth because her father is Kenyan.

A small child is found abandoned near a bus station in Eldoret with no identification or known parents. Because the child appears to be under eight years old and their nationality cannot be established, the Constitution presumes them to be a Kenyan citizen by birth.

sections.title: Citizenship by registration
sections.simplified: You can become a Kenyan citizen through registration if you have been married to a Kenyan for at least seven years, or if you have legally lived in Kenya continuously for seven or more years. Children adopted by Kenyans can also become citizens.
examples: Hassan, a Tanzanian national, has been married to a Kenyan woman for eight years and they live in Mombasa. He is now entitled to apply for Kenyan citizenship by registration — he does not have to renounce his Tanzanian citizenship to apply.

A Ugandan woman who has lived and worked legally in Nairobi for nine consecutive years now qualifies to apply for Kenyan citizenship by registration, provided she meets any additional conditions set by Parliament.

sections.title: Dual citizenship
sections.simplified: If you were born Kenyan, you can take up citizenship of another country without losing your Kenyan citizenship. You are free to hold both — Kenya recognises dual citizenship for citizens by birth.
examples: Otieno, born in Kisumu to Kenyan parents, moves to Canada and becomes a Canadian citizen after many years. He does not lose his Kenyan citizenship — he can hold both passports and travel on either.

A Kenyan woman studying in Germany is offered German citizenship after years of legal residence. She can accept it without worrying about losing her Kenyan citizenship, because she was born Kenyan and dual citizenship is constitutionally protected.

sections.title: Revocation of citizenship
sections.simplified: If you became a citizen through registration — not by birth — your citizenship can be taken away, but only under very specific serious circumstances like fraud, treason, or serious criminal convictions. Citizens by birth cannot have their citizenship revoked on these grounds.
examples: A foreign national who was registered as a Kenyan citizen is later found to have submitted forged documents during his application. His registration was obtained by fraud, so the government can legally revoke his citizenship.

A woman registered as a Kenyan citizen after marrying a Kenyan is convicted of treason five years after registration. This is one of the grounds that allows citizenship by registration to be revoked — she may lose her Kenyan citizenship.

sections.title: Legislation on citizenship
sections.simplified: Everything to do with citizenship — how you get it, how you give it up, how it can be taken away, and what rights and duties come with it — must be clearly set out in a law that Parliament passes. No government official can make up their own citizenship rules.
examples: A government officer tells an applicant that citizenship registration requires a personal payment to him directly. Because citizenship procedures must be set by Parliament in law, no unofficial requirements like personal payments are valid.

A Kenyan living abroad wants to formally renounce her citizenship. The Constitution requires Parliament to create a clear legal process for this — she is entitled to follow that process, not be subjected to arbitrary bureaucratic barriers.

chapters.title: THE BILL OF RIGHTS
chapters.fullTitle: Chapter Four – THE BILL OF RIGHTS
chapters.description: The rights and freedoms that protect you

sections.title: Rights and fundamental freedoms
sections.simplified: Your rights are not gifts from the government — they belong to you as a human being. The government did not create your rights and cannot simply decide to take them away. The Bill of Rights exists to protect what is already yours.
examples: A government minister announces that the right to peaceful protest is a 'privilege the government grants' and can be suspended when inconvenient. That is wrong — the Constitution makes clear rights belong to individuals, not the government to give or take.

Atieno is told by a local official that her right to access healthcare only applies when the government chooses to provide it. The Constitution says otherwise — rights are hers by virtue of being human, not at the pleasure of any official.

sections.title: Application of Bill of Rights
sections.simplified: The Bill of Rights applies everywhere — to every law, every government office, every court, and every person in Kenya. When a court is deciding a case involving your rights, they must always lean towards protecting those rights, not restricting them.
examples: A private company operating a school tells a student she cannot wear a hijab. Because the Bill of Rights binds all persons — not just government — the school cannot hide behind being 'private' to justify discriminating against her religious expression.

A magistrate is deciding a case where two possible interpretations of a law exist — one that restricts a person's freedom and one that protects it. The Constitution requires the magistrate to choose the interpretation that better protects the right.

sections.title: Implementation of rights and fundamental freedoms
sections.simplified: The government is not just required to avoid violating your rights — it has an active duty to make sure your rights are real and reachable. This means extra attention must go to groups that have historically been left out: women, older people, disabled people, children, youth, and marginalised communities.
examples: A county government builds a new public hospital but installs no ramps or accessible facilities for people using wheelchairs. This violates the State's duty to fulfil rights — specifically the rights of persons with disabilities — not just to avoid harming them.

The national government consistently fails to include women-led businesses in government procurement. Because the State has a duty to promote the rights of women as a vulnerable group, this systematic exclusion can be legally challenged.

sections.title: Enforcement of Bill of Rights
sections.simplified: You can take the government or anyone else to court if your rights are being violated — and you do not have to pay a court fee to start the case. You can also go to court on behalf of someone else who cannot do it themselves, or on behalf of a group of people facing the same violation.
examples: A human rights organisation discovers that police are routinely beating suspects during arrest across several police stations. Even though the organisation is not personally affected, it can go to court on behalf of those victims — and the Constitution says no filing fee can be charged for such a case.

Kamau's elderly mother cannot afford a lawyer and is too ill to appear in court after her land was illegally taken. Kamau can file court proceedings on her behalf to protect her property rights, without needing to prove he was personally harmed.

sections.title: Authority of courts to uphold and enforce the Bill of Rights
sections.simplified: The High Court has the power to step in whenever your rights are violated and provide real remedies — including stopping an unconstitutional action immediately, compensating you for harm suffered, or declaring an unjust law invalid.
examples: A community near a factory is suffering severe pollution affecting their health. They go to the High Court, which can issue an immediate conservatory order stopping the factory from operating until the rights violation is addressed — the court does not have to wait for a full trial.

Wanjiku proves in court that a government regulation unlawfully restricts women from accessing a certain type of business licence. The High Court can declare that regulation unconstitutional and void, ending the restriction for all women, not just Wanjiku.

sections.title: Limitation of rights and fundamental freedoms
sections.simplified: Your rights can only be restricted through a properly passed law, and only when there is a genuinely good reason that holds up under scrutiny. Even then, the restriction must be the smallest possible — the government cannot take more of your rights than the situation strictly requires.
examples: During a disease outbreak, the government restricts movement in a specific county to prevent spread. This is a permissible limitation — it is done through law, for a justifiable public health reason, and is limited to what is necessary.

A county government bans all political meetings indefinitely, claiming it is for 'public order.' This goes too far — the limitation on assembly must be specific, justifiable, and proportionate. An indefinite blanket ban without a specific reason does not meet the constitutional standard.

sections.title: Fundamental Rights and freedoms that may not be limited
sections.simplified: Some rights are absolute — they cannot be taken away by any law, emergency, or government decision. No matter what the circumstances, you cannot be tortured, enslaved, or denied a fair trial. These are floors below which Kenya can never go.
examples: During a state of emergency, police begin torturing detainees to extract confessions, claiming emergency powers justify it. They are wrong — the right to be free from torture cannot be suspended even during an emergency. It is an absolute right under the Constitution.

A person is held in prison for months without any trial or court appearance. They have an absolute right to apply for a habeas corpus order requiring the State to either charge them or release them — no law can take away this right.

sections.title: Right to life
sections.simplified: Every person's life is protected by law and cannot be taken intentionally without lawful justification. On abortion, the Constitution is specific — it is only permitted when a qualified health professional determines the mother's life or health is at serious risk, or in an emergency.
examples: Security forces shoot and kill unarmed protesters who pose no threat to anyone. That is an unlawful deprivation of life — the Constitution only permits lethal force in circumstances authorised by law, and killing peaceful protesters does not meet that standard.

A pregnant woman develops a life-threatening condition during pregnancy. Her doctor advises that continuing the pregnancy would endanger her life. Under this article, a medical termination is constitutionally permitted in that situation — the health professional's determination is the key.

sections.title: Equality and freedom from discrimination
sections.simplified: The law must treat every Kenyan the same — it cannot favour some people over others based on who they are, what they look like, or where they come from. The two-thirds gender rule also means any public body cannot be dominated by one gender.
examples: A bank refuses to give Atieno a loan because she is pregnant, claiming she is a financial risk. That is direct discrimination on the ground of pregnancy — it is unconstitutional and she can take legal action.

A county assembly is elected with 85% male members and 15% female members. This violates the two-thirds gender rule — the Constitution requires that no more than two-thirds of members of representative bodies be of the same gender.

sections.title: Human dignity
sections.simplified: You have dignity simply because you are human — not because of your wealth, status, job, or connections. No one — not the police, not an employer, not a government official — can treat you in a way that demeans or degrades you as a person.
examples: Prison wardens parade inmates before a crowd for humiliation as a form of punishment. This violates the right to human dignity — even people convicted of crimes retain their inherent dignity and cannot be treated in a degrading manner.

A hospital refuses to treat a patient until she cleans the ward floor herself, despite being seriously ill. This demeans her dignity as a human being and as a patient — it is a constitutional violation regardless of the hospital's internal rules.

sections.title: Freedom and security of the person
sections.simplified: You have the right to live without the threat of violence, arbitrary arrest, or any form of physical or psychological abuse — whether from the government or from private individuals. This applies whether you are at home, in custody, or anywhere else.
examples: A landlord physically assaults his tenant for being late with rent. Even though this is between private individuals, the Constitution's protection against violence applies — the tenant can report the assault and take legal action.

Police detain Kamau for three days without charging him or bringing him before a court, simply because he attended a political rally they disliked. This is arbitrary detention and a direct violation of his freedom and security of the person.

sections.title: Slavery, servitude and forced labour
sections.simplified: No one can own you, control your life as if you are property, or force you to work against your will. This applies to all forms of slavery — including modern forms like domestic servitude, debt bondage, or trafficking for labour.
examples: A domestic worker in Nairobi has her passport confiscated by her employer, is given no days off, and is told she must work until a debt to the employer is cleared. This is servitude and forced labour — both are unconstitutional and criminally prohibited.

A construction company threatens workers that they must work unpaid overtime or be blacklisted from future jobs. Forced labour does not only mean physical coercion — using threats to compel unpaid work is also a violation.

sections.title: Privacy
sections.simplified: Your home, your phone, your messages, your personal data, and your private life are yours. Nobody — not the government, police, employer, or private company — can search your home, seize your belongings, or pry into your communications without lawful authority.
examples: Police kick in Wanjiku's door at midnight without a search warrant and go through her belongings looking for her husband. Without a court-issued warrant, this search is unconstitutional — her home is protected under her right to privacy.

A company selling mobile phones secretly collects and sells users' private messages to third parties without consent. This infringes the constitutional right to privacy of communications — affected users can take legal action.

sections.title: Freedom of conscience, religion, belief and opinion
sections.simplified: Your beliefs and conscience are your own. You cannot be fired, denied a place in school, or blocked from any institution simply because of what you believe or which religion you follow. And no one can force you to do something that goes against your sincere religious convictions.
examples: A company refuses to hire a qualified candidate because he is Muslim and will need to pray during the workday. That is discrimination based on religion — it violates this right and the anti-discrimination provisions of Article 27.

A nurse is instructed by her hospital to assist in a procedure that directly contradicts her sincerely held religious beliefs. The Constitution protects her right not to be compelled to act against her religion, though the hospital may be entitled to reassign her while ensuring patient care continues.

sections.title: Freedom of expression
sections.simplified: You are free to speak, write, create, research, and share ideas without government interference. But free expression has limits — you cannot use it to spread hatred, incite people to violence, or push war propaganda. That is where free speech ends.
examples: Chebet writes and publishes a play sharply criticising the President's economic policies. The government cannot ban the play or arrest her for it — artistic and political expression is fully protected under this article.

A radio presenter repeatedly broadcasts statements calling members of a particular ethnic community criminals who deserve to be attacked. This is hate speech and incitement — it falls outside the protection of freedom of expression and is a criminal offence.

sections.title: Freedom of the media
sections.simplified: Media houses — TV, radio, newspapers, online outlets — are free from government control or interference. State-owned media like KBC must be balanced and independent, not mouthpieces for the ruling party. No politician or government official can dictate what gets published or broadcast.
examples: A cabinet minister calls the editor of a national newspaper and threatens to revoke their licence if they publish an investigative story about government corruption. This is direct interference with media freedom — it is unconstitutional and the editor can report it and proceed with publication.

The national broadcaster only airs government press releases and refuses to give airtime to opposition politicians or critical voices ahead of an election. This violates the constitutional requirement that State media be impartial and allow divergent views.

sections.title: Access to information
sections.simplified: You have a constitutional right to ask the government for information it holds, and they must respond. You also have the right to correct false information about yourself that is being held or spread. The government cannot keep important public information secret.
examples: Hassan submits a formal request to his county government asking for the budget allocation and expenditure records for his ward. The county government must provide this information — it is held by the State and he has a constitutional right to access it.

A hospital's records contain false information about Atieno that incorrectly states she has a communicable disease, which has damaged her employment prospects. She has the right to have that incorrect information corrected or deleted.

sections.title: Freedom of association
sections.simplified: You can join any group, club, union, society, or organisation you choose — and you cannot be forced to join one against your will. The government cannot unreasonably block you from registering a legitimate association, and they must give you a fair hearing before shutting one down.
examples: Wanjiku and other market vendors want to form a traders' association to collectively negotiate better stall fees. The government cannot arbitrarily refuse to register their association — refusal must be based on specific legal grounds and is subject to challenge.

An employer insists all workers must join a specific union and docks the pay of those who refuse. Forcing someone to join an association is unconstitutional — Kenyans have the right to freely choose whether or not to associate.

sections.title: Assembly, demonstration, picketing and petition
sections.simplified: You have the right to gather with others, march, protest, picket outside a building, and hand in petitions to government — as long as you are peaceful and unarmed. The police cannot simply disperse a peaceful protest because they dislike the message.
examples: University students stage a peaceful march to the education ministry to protest fee increases, carrying placards and chanting. Police order them to disperse without any legal justification. The students are exercising a constitutional right — the police order is unlawful.

Residents of a Nairobi estate picket outside their landlord's office to demand repairs of a broken water system. This is constitutionally protected picketing — the landlord cannot have them forcibly removed simply for standing peacefully outside.

sections.title: Political rights
sections.simplified: Your political choices are yours alone. You can form a party, join one, campaign for candidates, vote in private, or run for office yourself. No one can lawfully stop you from participating in democratic politics because of your views or background.
examples: A young woman from a small community wants to run for the county assembly. Local officials tell her she needs their endorsement to register as a candidate. That is false — the Constitution gives every adult citizen the right to stand for office without requiring approval from existing politicians.

An employer warns his workers that anyone who votes for the opposition will be fired. This is an attempt to interfere with the secret ballot and political freedom — it is unconstitutional and workers can report it without fear of retaliation.

sections.title: Freedom of movement and residence
sections.simplified: You are free to live, work, and move anywhere within Kenya. You can also leave the country and return. No government, county, or community can tell you that you are not allowed to live or move in a certain part of Kenya.
examples: A county governor announces that people from other counties are not welcome to settle or do business in his county and threatens to expel newcomers. That violates every Kenyan citizen's constitutional right to reside anywhere in the country.

Immigration officials at the airport confiscate Otieno's passport and tell him he cannot travel abroad. Restricting someone's right to leave the country can only be done through a specific legal process — arbitrary confiscation of a passport to prevent travel is unconstitutional.

sections.title: Protection of right to property
sections.simplified: You have the right to own property — land, a house, a business, anything — anywhere in Kenya. The government cannot take your property on a whim. If the government needs your land for a public project, they must pay you fair compensation promptly and you can challenge the process in court.
examples: The county government demolishes Wanjiku's business stall without notice, giving no reason and no compensation. This is an arbitrary deprivation of property — she is entitled to challenge it in court and claim compensation.

The national government acquires farmland in Nakuru to build a road. The farmers are constitutionally entitled to prompt payment of full and just compensation — they cannot be told to wait years for money while their land is already being used.

sections.title: Labour relations
sections.simplified: As a worker, you have real constitutional rights at your workplace — including the right to be paid fairly, work in decent conditions, join a union, and go on strike when necessary. Employers cannot punish you for unionising or striking lawfully.
examples: Factory workers in Athi River are paid below the legal minimum wage and are not allowed to form a workers' committee. Both are constitutional violations — the workers have the right to fair remuneration and to join or form a trade union.

Nurses at a public hospital go on strike to demand better working conditions. The government threatens to fire all striking nurses. This violates the constitutional right to strike — workers cannot be dismissed simply for exercising a constitutionally protected right.

sections.title: Environment
sections.simplified: A clean environment is your constitutional right — not a luxury. You can go to court to stop pollution, demand government action on environmental destruction, and insist that Kenya's natural resources are managed for future generations too, not just for profit today.
examples: A factory dumps toxic waste into a river used by a community for drinking water. The community members do not have to wait for the government to act — they can go directly to court under their constitutional right to a clean environment and demand the dumping stops.

A county government approves a mining licence for an area near a community forest without an environmental impact assessment. Residents can challenge the licence in court — their right to a healthy environment protects them from decisions that endanger their natural surroundings.

sections.title: Economic and social rights
sections.simplified: The Constitution guarantees you access to healthcare, housing, food, water, education, and social security. The most immediate of these is healthcare — no hospital can turn you away when it is an emergency, no matter your ability to pay.
examples: A woman in labour is turned away from a public hospital because she has no money for the facility levy. This is unconstitutional — no person can be denied emergency medical treatment. The hospital is legally required to stabilise and treat her immediately.

A county government consistently allocates zero funding to water access in a rural community, leaving residents to walk hours each day. The community can petition a court to compel the county to progressively work towards their right to clean, safe water.

sections.title: Language and culture
sections.simplified: You have the right to live your cultural life and speak your language freely. But culture cannot be forced on you — no one can compel you to participate in a cultural ritual or practice against your will, including practices done in the name of tradition.
examples: Parents insist that their daughter must undergo female genital cutting because it is a community tradition. The Constitution is clear — she cannot be compelled to undergo any cultural practice. Her bodily integrity and autonomy override cultural expectation.

A school teacher ridicules students for speaking their mother tongue during break time, insisting only English or Kiswahili may be spoken. Every person has the right to use the language of their choice — the teacher's restriction violates that right.

sections.title: Family
sections.simplified: Marriage is a constitutionally protected right that must be based on free and genuine consent — nobody can be forced into marriage. And once married, both partners have equal rights throughout — including at the end of the marriage when it comes to sharing property.
examples: A family arranges a marriage for their 17-year-old daughter without her knowledge or consent and pressures her to accept. This violates the constitutional requirement that marriage be based on free consent — she can refuse and seek legal protection.

After a divorce, Atieno's husband claims all jointly acquired property belongs to him alone as the man of the house. The Constitution gives equal rights to both spouses — she is entitled to an equal share of marital property.

sections.title: Consumer rights
sections.simplified: When you buy something or pay for a service, you have constitutional rights as a consumer. If a product is defective or a service falls short and causes you harm, you are entitled to compensation — not just a 'sorry.'
examples: Hassan buys a gas cooker that explodes after two weeks of normal use, injuring him. As a consumer, he has the right to compensation for that injury from the seller or manufacturer — defective products that cause harm trigger constitutional liability.

A mobile phone company advertises unlimited data but hides key limitations in tiny print. Consumers have a constitutional right to the information necessary to fully benefit from what they are paying for — misleading marketing can be legally challenged.

sections.title: Fair administrative action
sections.simplified: Any time a government body makes a decision that affects you — licensing, permits, termination, disciplinary action — they must do it fairly, promptly, and with good reason. If they take action that harms your interests, they must give you written reasons explaining why.
examples: A business owner's trading licence is cancelled by the county government with no explanation and no chance to respond. She has a constitutional right to written reasons for the decision and to have the process reviewed — the cancellation without cause and explanation is unlawful.

A civil servant is transferred to a remote station with no reason given, even though he recently filed a complaint about corruption in his department. Under this article, he is entitled to written reasons for the transfer, and can challenge it if it appears retaliatory.

sections.title: Access to justice
sections.simplified: Every Kenyan has the right to use the courts — and court fees cannot be so high that they effectively shut out ordinary people from seeking justice. The government must actively ensure that the legal system is reachable by everyone, not just the wealthy.
examples: A rural woman whose land has been grabbed cannot afford the court filing fees demanded by the local court. Under this article, those fees must be reasonable and must not be a barrier — if she cannot afford them, the State must find a way to ensure she can still access justice.

A court registry demands a fee to access court records that a person needs for their own ongoing case. If that fee is unreasonably high and effectively prevents them from preparing their defence, it violates the constitutional right of access to justice.

sections.title: Rights of arrested persons
sections.simplified: From the moment you are arrested, your rights are protected. You must be told why you are being arrested in a language you understand, you do not have to say anything, you have the right to call a lawyer, and you must appear before a court within 24 hours — not be left sitting in a cell for days.
examples: Police arrest Kamau and refuse to tell him what he is being charged with, keep him in a cell for three days without taking him to court, and repeatedly interrogate him through the night. Every one of these actions violates his constitutional rights as an arrested person.

A woman is arrested and immediately interrogated without being told she has the right to remain silent or to call a lawyer. Anything she says in that interrogation could later be challenged in court as inadmissible, because her constitutional rights were violated from the moment of arrest.

sections.title: Fair hearing
sections.simplified: You are innocent until proven guilty — full stop. If you are taken to court, you have the right to a fair trial with enough time and information to defend yourself, a lawyer of your choice (or one provided at State expense if needed), a free interpreter if you do not understand the language, and the right to appeal any conviction.
examples: A magistrate convicts Otieno on the same day he is brought to court without giving him time to prepare a defence or call witnesses. That conviction violates his constitutional right to adequate time and facilities to prepare a defence — it can be overturned on appeal.

A Somali-speaking man is prosecuted in a court where proceedings are conducted entirely in English and no interpreter is provided. His right to a fair trial is violated — he is entitled to a free interpreter so he can follow and participate in the proceedings.

sections.title: Rights of persons detained, held in custody or imprisoned
sections.simplified: Going to prison or being detained does not strip you of your human rights. You keep your rights — only those incompatible with the physical fact of being locked up are limited. You still have dignity, healthcare, and the right to challenge your detention in court.
examples: A remand prisoner in Nairobi is denied access to any medication for a chronic illness because prison officials say inmates do not get privileges. This is unconstitutional — the right to health does not become incompatible with detention, and the prisoner must receive medical treatment.

A person who believes they are being held unlawfully in police custody petitions the High Court for a habeas corpus order. The court must hear the petition and, if the detention is unlawful, order their immediate release — this right cannot be taken away even inside a detention facility.

sections.title: Interpretation of this Part
sections.simplified: Articles 53 to 57 give extra detail on how rights apply to specific groups — children, people with disabilities, youth, minorities, and older people. This elaboration does not take away or weaken any right. It only adds clarity and extra protection.
examples: Someone argues that because children's rights are listed separately in Article 53, children do not benefit from the general rights in the Bill of Rights. That interpretation is wrong — Article 52 makes clear that this Part adds to rights, never subtracts from them.

A court ruling appears to use the specific articles on minority rights as a reason to limit general equality protections for minority group members. This misreads the Constitution — the specific provisions are additional protections, not replacements for the general Bill of Rights.

sections.title: Children
sections.simplified: Every child in Kenya has strong constitutional protections. From birth they have a right to a name, nationality, food, shelter, healthcare, and free education. They cannot be put to work in dangerous conditions, subjected to violence or abuse, or detained except when absolutely unavoidable — and in all decisions, what is best for the child comes first.
examples: A 12-year-old boy is pulled out of school to work full time in a quarry by his guardian. This violates his right to free and compulsory basic education and his protection from hazardous and exploitative labour — both are constitutional rights that can be enforced.

During a custody dispute, a court must decide where a child will live. Under this article, the decision must be made based on what is best for the child — not what is most convenient for either parent, and not on the basis of gender alone.

sections.title: Persons with disabilities
sections.simplified: Kenyans living with disabilities are not second-class citizens. They have the right to access public places, transport, education, and information just like everyone else. Government offices, schools, and public spaces must be accessible. And at least 5% of seats in public bodies must be held by people with disabilities.
examples: A public hospital is newly constructed with no ramps, no accessible toilets, and no sign language interpreters. This violates the constitutional right of persons with disabilities to reasonable access to places and services — the hospital must be redesigned to be accessible.

A county assembly appoints 40 members but none of them is a person with disability. The Constitution requires at least 5% of such bodies to be persons with disabilities — the assembly is in violation and the appointment process can be legally challenged.

sections.title: Youth
sections.simplified: Young Kenyans between 18 and 35 years are constitutionally recognised as a group requiring special support. The government must create real opportunities for youth in education, jobs, and political participation — not just lip service to youth empowerment.
examples: A county government creates a youth development fund but sets the minimum age of applicants at 35, which is the upper limit of 'youth' under the Constitution. This effectively excludes most young people — the fund is supposed to target those who are young, not those approaching middle age.

A 24-year-old woman from a marginalised community is excluded from a community leadership forum because 'young women don't speak in public.' The Constitution requires that youth, including young women, have opportunities to participate in social and governance spheres.

sections.title: Minorities and marginalised groups
sections.simplified: Communities and groups that have historically been left out — because of their small numbers, location, or disadvantaged history — are entitled to special government programmes designed to include them. This is not charity; it is a constitutional obligation.
examples: A pastoralist community in a remote area has no government schools within 100km. The Constitution requires the State to take affirmative action to ensure marginalised communities access education — the government must find ways to bring schooling to that community.

A small indigenous community in a forest area has no representative in any county or national body and is never consulted on decisions affecting their land. The State is constitutionally obligated to put programmes in place to ensure their participation and representation in governance.

sections.title: Older members of society
sections.simplified: Kenyans aged 60 and above have specific constitutional protections. They cannot be cast aside, neglected, or abused. The State must ensure they can continue participating in society and that both family and government provide them with care and support.
examples: An elderly man is evicted from his home by his adult children who want to sell the property, and he is left with no means of support. Under this article, he has the right to reasonable care and assistance from his family, and the State must intervene to protect him from abuse and destitution.

A county government's social protection programme consistently excludes older residents from its cash transfer scheme because they are seen as 'unproductive.' The Constitution requires the State to ensure the welfare and dignity of older persons — that exclusion is unconstitutional.

sections.title: State of emergency
sections.simplified: The President can declare a state of emergency in a genuine crisis — but it cannot last forever and Parliament must approve any extension. Even during an emergency, absolute rights like freedom from torture cannot be touched.
examples: A President declares a state of emergency during floods and extends it indefinitely without going back to Parliament. This is unconstitutional — extensions require Parliament's approval with two-thirds majority after 14 days, and no extension can exceed two months at a time.

During a state of emergency, security forces begin torturing detainees, claiming emergency powers allow it. Torture is one of the absolute rights that cannot be limited under any circumstances — not even during a declared state of emergency.

sections.title: Kenya National Human Rights and Equality Commission
sections.simplified: The Kenya National Human Rights and Equality Commission is the official body you can complain to if your rights are being violated — by government, employers, or anyone else. They investigate, make recommendations, and can push for remedies without you having to go to court.
examples: Wanjiku is repeatedly passed over for promotion at her government job despite better qualifications than male colleagues who are promoted. She can file a complaint with the Kenya National Human Rights and Equality Commission, which will investigate and can compel the employer to account for the discrimination.

A group of residents allege that police in their area routinely beat up young men from their community for no reason. They can report the pattern to the Commission, which has the mandate to investigate human rights abuses and recommend action against offending officers.

chapters.title: LAND AND ENVIRONMENT
chapters.description: How land, housing, and nature are protected

sections.title: Principles of land policy
sections.simplified: Land in Kenya must be managed fairly for everyone — not just for those with money or connections. Women must have the same access to land as men, land administration must be transparent, and communities should be empowered to solve land disputes locally.
examples: A widow is prevented from inheriting her late husband's land by his brothers, based on a community custom that women cannot hold land. The Constitution explicitly eliminates gender discrimination in land — she has an equal right to that land and can enforce it in court.

A county land office is allocating plots based on political connections rather than need or a transparent process. The Constitution requires transparent and cost-effective land administration — this process can be legally challenged.

sections.title: Classification of land
sections.simplified: At the most fundamental level, all land in Kenya belongs to Kenyans. It is then divided into three types: land held by the national government for everyone (public), land held by specific communities (community), and land owned by individuals or companies (private).
examples: A government official claims a piece of unregistered forestland is his personal property. Under the Constitution, unregistered land with no established owner is public land — it belongs to all Kenyans, not to any individual official.

A community in the Rift Valley has grazed cattle on the same land for generations but it has never been formally registered. The Constitution recognises community land — they have the right to have that land registered as belonging to their community.

sections.title: Public land
sections.simplified: Public land belongs to all Kenyans. Rivers, lakes, forests, game reserves, national parks, and roads are public land — they cannot be sold or given away privately. The National Land Commission manages this land on behalf of the people.
examples: A county governor signs an agreement giving a private company exclusive access to a public river for commercial use, excluding local communities. The river is public land held in trust for all Kenyans — it cannot be privately controlled without proper legal process and cannot exclude communities.

Residents discover that a government official has registered a national park buffer zone as private land in his name. Public land cannot be alienated to private individuals without following the constitutional process — the registration is invalid.

sections.title: Community land
sections.simplified: Land that has belonged to a community for generations — their grazing areas, sacred sites, ancestral home, community forests — is constitutionally recognised as theirs. It cannot be taken away and converted to private or public use without proper legal process.
examples: A government agency surveys and allocates to private developers the traditional grazing lands of a pastoralist community without their consent. This land is community land under the Constitution — it cannot be alienated without following the legal process, which must involve the community.

A community's ancestral forest has been used by them for generations but was never formally registered. Under this article, it qualifies as community land held in trust by the county government — the community can begin the process of having it formally registered as theirs.

sections.title: Private land
sections.simplified: Private land is land that an individual or company legally owns and holds a title deed for — whether as permanent freehold ownership or as a long-term lease. Your title deed is your proof of private land ownership.
examples: Kamau holds a title deed for a plot in Kiambu under freehold tenure. That is constitutionally protected private land — the government or individuals cannot simply take it from him without following the proper legal process including paying compensation.

A company holds a 50-year leasehold over industrial land in Nairobi. This is private land for the duration of the lease — during that period, they have legal rights over it just as a freehold owner would, within the terms of the lease.

sections.title: Landholding by non-citizens
sections.simplified: Foreigners cannot own Kenyan land permanently. They can only lease it — for a maximum of 99 years. If a company is not entirely owned by Kenyans, it is treated as a foreigner for purposes of land ownership and cannot hold freehold title.
examples: A British investor wants to buy a farm in the Rift Valley and hold freehold title. He cannot — as a non-citizen, he is limited to leasehold of up to 99 years. Any sale agreement purporting to give him freehold is constitutionally invalid.

A company registered in Kenya but 60% owned by Chinese investors applies for freehold land. Because it is not wholly owned by Kenyan citizens, it is treated as a non-citizen for land purposes and can only hold land on a leasehold not exceeding 99 years.

sections.title: Regulation of land use and property
sections.simplified: The government can regulate how land is used — for example through zoning, environmental rules, or public safety requirements — without being required to compensate you for those regulations. But investments in property must also benefit local communities, not just outside investors.
examples: A county government rezones an industrial area for residential use, which affects the value of a factory owner's property. This is a valid land use regulation — the owner cannot demand compensation for a lawful rezoning decision that serves planning purposes.

A foreign company gets approval to build a large real estate development in Kisumu but the project creates no local jobs and benefits go entirely offshore. The Constitution requires Parliament to ensure investments in property benefit local communities — this project should be reviewed.

sections.title: National Land Commission
sections.simplified: The National Land Commission manages all public land in Kenya and investigates historical land injustices — including land that was taken unfairly under colonial rule or since independence. If your community believes land was unjustly taken from them, the Commission can investigate and recommend how to make it right.
examples: A community in the Coast province claims that their land was illegally alienated during the colonial period and has never been returned. They can petition the National Land Commission to investigate the historical injustice and recommend appropriate redress.

Two communities in western Kenya have a long-running land boundary dispute. The National Land Commission can encourage the use of traditional dispute resolution mechanisms as an alternative to costly and slow court proceedings.

sections.title: Legislation on land
sections.simplified: Parliament is required to clean up and modernise all land laws — including setting limits on how much land one person can own, protecting the family home during and after marriage, and ensuring dependent family members are not left landless when a relative dies.
examples: A powerful landowner in Trans Nzoia owns hundreds of thousands of acres while neighbouring communities have no land. Parliament is constitutionally mandated to set maximum land holding limits that would make such extreme concentration of land illegal.

A woman is evicted from the family home immediately after her husband dies, even though she has lived there for decades. Legislation required by this article must protect dependants' rights to land and the matrimonial home after the death of a spouse.

sections.title: Obligations in respect of the environment
sections.simplified: The government must protect Kenya's environment for future generations — including maintaining at least 10% tree cover across the country. Every Kenyan also has a personal duty to help protect the environment. Environmental damage is not just a political issue — it is a constitutional one.
examples: The government approves a large project that will clear thousands of hectares of forest, reducing tree cover below the constitutional 10% threshold. Citizens can go to court to stop this — the State has a constitutional duty to maintain that minimum tree cover.

A factory dumps chemicals into a river without an environmental impact assessment being conducted first. Under this article, the State must have established systems to review environmental impact before any activity that might harm the environment — skipping the assessment is unconstitutional.

sections.title: Enforcement of environmental rights
sections.simplified: You can go to court to protect the environment even if you personally have not been harmed yet. You just need to show there is a threat to the environment or a violation of environmental rights. You do not have to wait until the damage is done.
examples: Residents near a proposed quarry site believe the blasting will destroy a nearby water source. They can file a court application before blasting even begins — you do not have to wait for the damage to happen to enforce environmental rights.

A public officer is knowingly approving illegal logging in a protected forest. Any person can apply to court to compel that officer to stop approving illegal activities — without having to prove they personally suffered harm.

sections.title: Agreements relating to natural resources
sections.simplified: Any contract that gives a company or individual the right to extract Kenya's natural resources — oil, minerals, timber, fish — must be approved by Parliament. The President or government cannot secretly sign away Kenya's resources without parliamentary oversight.
examples: The government secretly signs a 30-year oil extraction agreement with a foreign company, granting them exclusive rights over a large oil block. Because this involves exploitation of a natural resource, Parliament must ratify it — the deal is not legally binding until Parliament approves it.

A mining company begins operating in northern Kenya based on a concession given by a ministry official alone. Without parliamentary ratification, that concession does not comply with the Constitution — the company's operations can be legally challenged.

sections.title: Legislation relating to the environment
sections.simplified: Parliament must pass laws to make the environmental protections in the Constitution real and enforceable — not leave them as aspirational words. Environmental legislation must give practical teeth to the rights and obligations set out in this chapter.
examples: Citizens notice that there are no clear legal penalties for companies that violate environmental rules because Parliament has not yet passed detailed environmental enforcement legislation. Under this article, Parliament has a constitutional duty to fill that gap.

A court is asked to enforce an environmental right but finds no legislation specifying how to do so. This triggers Parliament's obligation under this article — environmental rights need enabling legislation to be fully effective.

sections.title: Responsibilities of leadership
sections.simplified: Every State officer — from the President to a ward administrator — holds public power as a trust on behalf of citizens, not as their personal property. Their job is to serve, not to dominate. Corruption, favouritism, and self-serving behaviour all betray this constitutional trust.
examples: A cabinet secretary uses government vehicles and staff to campaign for their political party during working hours. This is a misuse of public authority — it serves their personal political interest rather than the people, violating the leadership responsibilities in this article.

A county official awards all government contracts to members of his ethnic community, regardless of merit. This violates the constitutional requirement that leaders be objective, impartial, and not influenced by nepotism — it is a betrayal of public trust.

sections.title: Oath of office of State officers
sections.simplified: Every State officer — including MPs, judges, the President — must swear an oath or make a formal affirmation before they can exercise any power. Taking that oath is a solemn commitment to serve the Constitution and the people of Kenya.
examples: A newly elected county governor begins issuing directives and signing official documents before being sworn in. Any actions taken before the oath of office are constitutionally invalid — the oath is a prerequisite to lawfully exercising State power.

An MP refuses to take the prescribed oath, insisting on his own version. The Constitution is specific about the form of the oath — an MP who does not take it as required cannot lawfully sit or vote in Parliament.

sections.title: Conduct of State officers
sections.simplified: State officers cannot put their personal financial interests or private agenda above their public duties. If caught doing so, they can be dismissed and permanently barred from ever holding any other State office.
examples: A county executive committee member awards a supply contract to her own company while chairing the procurement meeting. This is a direct conflict between her personal financial interest and her public duty — it is grounds for dismissal under this article.

A Principal Secretary who is dismissed for misconduct applies for appointment as a commissioner a year later. Because the dismissal was for the conduct described in this article, he is constitutionally disqualified from holding any other State office.

sections.title: Financial probity of State officers
sections.simplified: Gifts given to government officials at official functions belong to Kenya, not to the official personally. State officers also cannot keep bank accounts abroad or accept loans and benefits that create obligations or compromises to their public duties.
examples: A foreign dignitary presents a valuable painting to a Cabinet Secretary during a State function. The painting belongs to the Republic of Kenya — the Cabinet Secretary must hand it over to the State and cannot keep it at home as a personal gift.

A governor accepts a private loan from a company that regularly bids for county tenders. Even if repaid, this creates a conflict of interest that compromises his integrity — it is a violation of this article and grounds for disciplinary action.

sections.title: Restriction on activities of State officers
sections.simplified: Full-time government officers cannot hold other paid jobs — their public duty demands their full attention. Appointed officers must stay out of party politics. And retired officers on a government pension cannot take on too many other paid government roles.
examples: A Principal Secretary quietly maintains a paid directorship in a private company while serving in government. This is prohibited — full-time State officers cannot participate in other gainful employment while in office.

A retired judge receiving a government pension sits as the paid chairperson of three different State corporations simultaneously. The Constitution limits retired officers receiving a pension to a maximum of two concurrent remunerative positions in State entities.

sections.title: Citizenship and leadership
sections.simplified: You must be a Kenyan citizen to hold any State office. And if you are already a State officer or in the military, you cannot hold citizenship of another country at the same time — unless you were given it automatically by another country's law and had no choice.
examples: A person who recently became a naturalised Kenyan citizen after living in Kenya for years wants to run for a county assembly seat. Provided they are a citizen at the time of the election, they are eligible — citizenship is the key requirement.

An army officer discovers he automatically became a citizen of a foreign country by descent following a change in that country's citizenship law, without any action on his part. The Constitution exempts cases where citizenship was acquired by operation of another country's law without ability to opt out — his position is protected.

sections.title: Legislation to establish the ethics and anti-corruption commission
sections.simplified: The Constitution requires Parliament to create an independent ethics and anti-corruption body to enforce the leadership and integrity rules. This commission must be independent — it cannot be controlled by the politicians it is supposed to watch.
examples: A politician is accused of misusing public funds and the public wants to know why the anti-corruption commission has not investigated. The Constitution mandates Parliament to establish this commission with real independence and enforcement powers — citizens can demand it be functional and resourced.

The government attempts to put the ethics commission under the control of the Office of the President, making it report to the President rather than Parliament. This undermines its constitutional independence — the commission must be independent to enforce integrity rules against all State officers.

sections.title: Legislation on leadership
sections.simplified: Parliament must pass laws to make the Chapter 6 leadership and integrity requirements enforceable — including clear penalties for breaches that go beyond what the Constitution already spells out.
examples: A county official breaches the integrity rules but there is no clear legal penalty because Parliament has not yet enacted the required legislation. This highlights Parliament's constitutional duty — without it, enforcement is weakened and violators may escape accountability.

A civil society organisation petitions Parliament to speed up passing of integrity laws affecting public officers below State officer level. Article 80 explicitly requires Parliament to extend these protections to public officers generally — not just top State officials.

chapters.title: REPRESENTATION OF THE PEOPLE
chapters.fullTitle: Chapter Seven – REPRESENTATION OF THE PEOPLE
chapters.description: How citizens choose and replace leaders

sections.title: General principles for the electoral system
sections.simplified: Kenya's elections must be genuinely free and fair — your vote must be secret, the process must be transparent, and no single gender can dominate elective bodies. If those standards are not met, the election can be challenged in court.
examples: Party agents at a polling station are allowed inside the voting booth area, compromising the secrecy of the ballot. This directly violates the constitutional requirement for a secret ballot — the election results in that station can be challenged.

A county assembly is elected with 68% of members being male. This exceeds the two-thirds gender rule — the result violates the constitutional principle and the IEBC must take corrective measures through the party list system.

sections.title: Legislation on elections
sections.simplified: Parliament must pass laws covering every aspect of elections — from how constituencies are drawn to how Kenyans in the diaspora can vote. These are not optional — if Parliament fails to enact them, it can be compelled to do so.
examples: Kenyan citizens living and working in Australia want to vote in the general election but no system exists for diaspora voting. The Constitution requires Parliament to progressively implement their right to vote — the delay in creating that system is a failure of a constitutional obligation.

A candidate is nominated by a party under unclear procedures that were never defined in law. Because Parliament is required to enact clear nomination laws, the lack of clear rules can be grounds for a legal challenge to the nomination process.

sections.title: Registration as a voter
sections.simplified: Every adult Kenyan citizen who is mentally sound and has not committed an election offence in the last five years has the right to be registered as a voter. No administrative barriers — long queues, inconvenient locations, complicated processes — can be used to effectively deny eligible citizens that right.
examples: The IEBC sets up voter registration centres only in urban areas, making it impossible for millions of rural Kenyans to register. The Constitution requires administrative arrangements to facilitate voter registration, not obstruct it — this arrangement can be legally challenged.

A person who was convicted of voter bribery three years ago attempts to register as a voter. She is disqualified — election offences within the preceding five years are one of the specific grounds for disqualification from registration.

sections.title: Candidates for election and political parties to comply with code of conduct
sections.simplified: No candidate or political party is above the IEBC's election rules. They must all follow the code of conduct — which covers campaigning ethics, prohibited conduct, and the handling of electoral materials.
examples: A powerful political party ignores the IEBC code of conduct by campaigning in school compounds and distributing cash to voters. The code is constitutionally binding — the IEBC can take action against both the party and its candidates for violating it.

An independent candidate uses hate speech in his campaign materials, which violates the IEBC code of conduct. The IEBC has authority to disqualify candidates who breach the code — compliance is not optional for anyone seeking election.

sections.title: Eligibility to stand as an independent candidate
sections.simplified: You can run for office without belonging to a political party — but you must have genuinely left any party at least three months before the election. You cannot leave a party a week before the elections and claim to be an independent candidate.
examples: A popular community leader resigns from her political party two months before the election deadline and tries to register as an independent candidate. She is not eligible — the Constitution requires at least three months of non-membership before the election.

A young man who has never joined any political party decides to run for his ward's county assembly seat as an independent candidate. He meets the eligibility requirement — he is not and has never been a member of a registered party.

sections.title: Voting
sections.simplified: The IEBC must run elections that are technically sound, transparent, and quick. Results must be announced promptly at each polling station — not delayed or hidden. The process from voting to final announcement must be fully observable and verifiable.
examples: After polls close, returning officers in several constituencies delay announcing results for days while handling ballot boxes in secret locations. The Constitution requires results to be announced promptly and the collation to be open — this delay and secrecy violates the law.

The IEBC introduces a new electronic voting system that cannot be independently verified and produces no paper trail. The Constitution requires the system to be verifiable and transparent — a system that cannot be audited fails this constitutional standard.

sections.title: Electoral disputes
sections.simplified: If you believe an election was flawed, you have exactly 28 days from the announcement of results to file a petition in court. Miss that window and you lose your right to challenge the results, no matter how strong your evidence.
examples: Atieno believes the National Assembly election in her constituency was stolen through ballot stuffing. She has 28 days from the day the IEBC declared the winner to file an election petition in the High Court — after that, her case cannot be heard.

A voter who witnessed irregularities waits two months before trying to file an election petition, thinking the 28-day period is just a guideline. The court dismisses his case immediately — the 28-day window is a constitutional deadline, not a suggestion.

sections.title: Independent Electoral and Boundaries Commission
sections.simplified: The IEBC runs Kenya's elections — independently, without interference from government or political parties. It also draws constituency boundaries, educates voters, and resolves disputes before results are declared. Its independence is constitutionally guaranteed.
examples: The President issues a directive instructing the IEBC to postpone the election by two months. The IEBC is constitutionally independent — the President has no authority to give it directions on how to run elections, and the commission can refuse and proceed.

A political party claims the IEBC unfairly redrew constituency boundaries to disadvantage them. The IEBC's boundary delimitation process must follow constitutional criteria and can be challenged in the High Court within 30 days of publication.

sections.title: Delimitation of electoral units
sections.simplified: Kenya has 290 constituencies and the boundaries must be reviewed regularly to reflect population changes. No review can happen within 12 months of an election — and the boundaries must ensure each constituency represents roughly equal numbers of people.
examples: The IEBC announces new constituency boundaries just eight months before a general election. This violates the constitutional requirement that any review be completed at least 12 months before an election — the new boundaries cannot apply to the upcoming election.

A community notes that their constituency has three times the population of a neighbouring one, meaning their votes count for far less. The IEBC's boundary review must address this — the Constitution requires boundaries to reflect as equal a population quota as possible.

sections.title: Allocation of party list seats
sections.simplified: The special seats in Parliament (for youth, women, persons with disabilities) are allocated to parties based on how many seats they won in the general election. Parties must alternate men and women on their lists — not put all women at the bottom.
examples: A political party submits a party list for nominated seats with 10 men listed first and 2 women at the very end, knowing they will only qualify for 5 seats. This violates the constitutional requirement for alternation between male and female candidates — the list must alternate properly.

A party that wins 30% of elected seats in the National Assembly is entitled to 30% of the party list seats — not more, not less. The proportional representation principle ensures party list allocation matches actual voter support.

sections.title: Basic requirements for political parties
sections.simplified: Political parties cannot be tribal, religious, or regional clubs. They must be open to all Kenyans, hold internal elections, respect human rights, and refuse to use violence or corruption. A party built around one ethnic group or religion is constitutionally invalid.
examples: A group tries to register a political party openly named after a specific ethnic community, with membership restricted to people from that community. This violates the constitutional requirement that parties have a national character — the Registrar of Political Parties must reject it.
A political party in a county covertly pays youth to disrupt an opponent's rallies. Encouraging violence is a constitutional prohibition — the party can be deregistered for this conduct.

sections.title: Legislation on political parties
sections.simplified: Parliament must pass laws ensuring political parties are properly regulated, funded fairly, and prevented from misusing government resources. State media must give all parties fair airtime — not just the ruling party.
examples: The national broadcaster gives the ruling party ten times more airtime than opposition parties during the election campaign period. The Constitution requires equitable airtime allocation — opposition parties can legally demand equal access to State media.

A Cabinet Secretary is found using a government vehicle and ministry staff to campaign for his party. The Constitution requires Parliament to restrict use of public resources for party promotion — this conduct is illegal and grounds for disciplinary action.

sections.title: Establishment of Parliament
sections.simplified: Kenya's Parliament is made up of two Houses — the National Assembly (for the people) and the Senate (for the counties). Each has its own role, and neither can completely replace the other.
examples: The ruling party passes a bill that affects county revenues through the National Assembly alone, bypassing the Senate. This is unconstitutional — bills affecting counties must go through both Houses, and the Senate exists specifically to protect county interests.

An MP claims the National Assembly has the power to overrule the Senate on all matters. This is wrong — both Houses have defined constitutional roles, and on matters concerning counties, the Senate has real power that the National Assembly cannot simply override.

sections.title: Role of Parliament
sections.simplified: Only Parliament can make laws for Kenya — no other body has that power unless Parliament specifically delegates it. Parliament does not make laws based on its own wishes — it represents and acts on behalf of the Kenyan people.
examples: The President issues an executive order that creates new taxes without Parliamentary approval. Only Parliament has the power to impose taxes — executive orders cannot create new financial obligations on citizens without legislative authority.

A government agency starts issuing regulations with the force of law without any Act of Parliament giving them that authority. This violates Article 94 — only Parliament can grant law-making power to other bodies, and it must be done explicitly.

sections.title: Role of the National Assembly
sections.simplified: Your MP in the National Assembly is there to speak for you, pass laws, control how government money is spent, and hold the President accountable. They can even approve or reject declarations of war — it is not a role to be taken lightly.
