examples: The national government announces it is spending billions on a project that was not in the approved budget. The National Assembly has the constitutional power to demand accountability — this is their oversight role and they can call ministers to explain.

Citizens in a constituency feel their MP never raises their concerns in Parliament, never votes on legislation, and is never in Nairobi. The MP is constitutionally required to deliberate on and resolve issues of concern to the people they represent — the electorate can recall an MP who fails in this duty.

sections.title: Role of the Senate
sections.simplified: Every county has senators in Nairobi specifically to defend county interests. If national legislation threatens county powers or money, the Senate must approve it. The Senate also plays a role in determining how much money each county gets.
examples: The national government passes a law reducing county revenue allocation below 15% of national revenue without Senate involvement. The Senate is constitutionally required to be involved in such decisions — the law is invalid without Senate participation.

The National Assembly votes to impeach the President. The Senate must then hold its own independent hearing — the President can only be removed if two-thirds of all senators vote to uphold the charges, giving the Senate a crucial check on presidential removal.

sections.title: Membership of the National Assembly
sections.simplified: The National Assembly has 349 members. Your direct representatives are the constituency MP you elect. On top of that, each county elects a woman MP, and parties nominate members to represent youth, disabled people, and workers — ensuring voices that might otherwise be left out are present.
examples: A voter in Kisumu county does not realise she can vote for both her constituency MP and a county woman representative. These are two separate votes for two different seats in the National Assembly — both are constitutionally provided for.

A party nominates only middle-aged men to the 12 nominated seats meant to represent youth, persons with disabilities, and workers. The Constitution specifies these seats are for those special interests — nominating people who don't represent them defeats the constitutional purpose.

sections.title: Membership of the Senate
sections.simplified: The Senate has 67 members. Every county elects one senator directly. Beyond that, parties nominate women senators, youth representatives, and senators with disabilities — ensuring the Senate reflects the diversity of Kenyan society.
examples: Residents of Narok county go to vote for their Senator alongside other county elections. Each county is one constituency for Senate purposes — Narok's residents elect one Senator to represent their county's interests in the upper house.

A political party that wins 40% of the Senate elected seats gets 40% of the 16 nominated women's seats. The allocation is proportional to actual elected performance — parties cannot claim more nominated seats than their proportional share.

sections.title: Qualifications and disqualifications for election as member of Parliament
sections.simplified: You can run for Parliament if you are a registered voter, meet the educational and integrity requirements, and get enough voter signatures to support your nomination. But several things will disqualify you — including currently holding a public service job, being bankrupt, or serving a prison sentence.
examples: A civil servant working in the Kenya Revenue Authority wants to contest a parliamentary seat in the upcoming election. He must resign from the KRA before contesting — sitting public officers are constitutionally disqualified from running for Parliament.

A businesswoman who was declared bankrupt two years ago and whose case is still unresolved wants to run for a National Assembly seat. She is disqualified — undischarged bankrupts cannot stand for Parliament under the Constitution.

sections.title: Promotion of representation of marginalised groups
sections.simplified: Parliament must actively legislate to ensure that women, young people, disabled persons, and minority and marginalised communities have real representation in Parliament — not just on paper, but in practice.
examples: Parliament passes a bill increasing the number of special seats for persons with disabilities in both houses. This is fulfilling the constitutional mandate to promote representation — the legislation is required, not optional.

Civil society organisations petition Parliament to enact the legislation required under this article after years of inaction. A court can order Parliament to fulfil this constitutional duty — failure to legislate can be legally challenged.

sections.title: Election of members of Parliament
sections.simplified: Parliamentary elections happen every five years on a set date — the second Tuesday in August. If an MP dies or vacates their seat, a by-election must be held quickly to fill the gap. But no by-elections happen in the three months just before a general election.
examples: An MP dies 10 months before the general election. A by-election must be held within 90 days to fill the seat — the constituency cannot go unrepresented for the remaining period before the general election.

An MP dies just two months before the scheduled general election. In this case, no by-election will be held — the vacancy falls within the three-month window before the general election, so the seat remains empty until the general election.

sections.title: Term of Parliament
sections.simplified: Parliament lasts for five years. It can only be extended — and only during wartime — by a two-thirds majority of both Houses, and even then, for no more than 12 months in total. A President cannot extend Parliament unilaterally.
examples: A President announces that due to a security crisis, he is extending Parliament's term by 18 months without seeking Parliament's approval. This is unconstitutional — only Parliament itself can vote to extend its term, only during actual war, and by a two-thirds majority.

Kenya is formally at war with a neighbouring state, and Parliament votes to extend its own term by 6 months so the country does not hold an election during active conflict. This is constitutionally permitted — provided the two-thirds majority threshold is met in both Houses.

sections.title: Vacation of office of member of Parliament
sections.simplified: An MP can lose their seat for multiple reasons — including switching parties. If you were elected on a party ticket and you leave that party, your seat goes with it. If you ran as an independent and then join a party, you also lose your seat.
examples: An MP elected on the ODM ticket publicly resigns from ODM and declares support for a rival party. Under the Constitution, leaving the party you were elected on means you lose your parliamentary seat — the vacancy must be filled.

An MP is absent from Parliament for 10 consecutive sittings without seeking the Speaker's written permission and provides no acceptable explanation. The seat is declared vacant — MPs have a constitutional duty to actually attend and participate in Parliament.

sections.title: Right of recall
sections.simplified: Voters have the constitutional right to remove an MP before their term ends, if the MP fails them badly enough. Parliament must pass a law spelling out the grounds and procedure for doing this — but the right itself is in the Constitution.
examples: Residents of a constituency are furious that their MP has not visited the constituency once in three years, never raises local issues in Parliament, and is embroiled in corruption. They have a constitutional right to initiate a recall process — provided Parliament has enacted the required legislation specifying how.

An MP who is recalled before completing their term is not entitled to their full benefits as if they served the complete term. The recall is the people's mechanism to hold lazy or corrupt MPs accountable between elections.

sections.title: Determination of questions of membership
sections.simplified: If you believe an election was rigged or that an MP has lost their seat, you take it to the High Court — and the court must hear and decide your case within six months. Justice delayed in election matters is justice denied.
examples: An election petition challenging the National Assembly results in a constituency is filed in the High Court. By law, the court must determine the case within six months — it cannot sit on the petition indefinitely while the potentially wrongly elected MP continues to serve.

A political party argues that one of their MPs automatically lost his seat when he was convicted of a crime, but the MP refuses to vacate. The High Court is the constitutionally designated body to make the final determination on whether the seat has become vacant.

sections.title: Speakers and Deputy Speakers of Parliament
sections.simplified: Each House of Parliament has a Speaker who chairs proceedings and a Deputy Speaker who fills in when needed. The Speaker must be someone qualified to be an MP but cannot be a current sitting member — they must be an independent presiding officer.
examples: A sitting MP is nominated to become Speaker of the National Assembly. She must first vacate her constituency seat before assuming the role — the Constitution requires the Speaker to be someone qualified to be an MP but not a current sitting member.

The Speaker of the Senate is absent during an important vote. The Deputy Speaker, who is a sitting Senator, presides over the session — this is exactly the constitutional role the Deputy Speaker exists to fill.

sections.title: Presiding in Parliament
sections.simplified: Parliamentary sessions always have a presiding officer — the Speaker, then the Deputy Speaker, then a member chosen by the House. At joint sittings of both Houses, the National Assembly Speaker is in charge.
examples: Both the Speaker and Deputy Speaker of the Senate are absent during an emergency session called to debate a crisis. The Senate must elect one of its sitting members to preside for that session — proceedings cannot go ahead without a presiding officer.
Parliament holds a joint sitting to hear the President's State of the Nation address. The Speaker of the National Assembly chairs the joint session — the Senate Speaker assists but does not lead.

sections.title: Party leaders
sections.simplified: The National Assembly has a formal power structure — the Speaker presides over everyone, then the majority party leader who leads the ruling side, then the opposition leader. This ensures a structured check between government and opposition.
examples: After a coalition reshuffles its numbers in the National Assembly, a previously minority party now commands more seats than the ruling coalition. The leader of that party should become the new majority leader — the constitutional title follows actual numbers, not political convenience.

The government attempts to bypass the minority leader when scheduling debates on important legislation. The minority leader's role is constitutionally recognised — they have specific procedural rights and cannot simply be ignored in parliamentary proceedings.

sections.title: Exercise of legislative powers
sections.simplified: Laws are made through a clear process: Parliament passes a bill, and the President gives it assent. Most bills only go through the National Assembly. Bills that affect county governments must go through both Houses.
examples: Parliament passes a bill on immigration — a national matter with no county government implications. It only needs to go through the National Assembly and receive presidential assent — the Senate is not involved in this type of legislation.

A bill setting out how county revenue funds shall be managed is introduced in the National Assembly. Because it concerns county governments, it must also pass through the Senate before it can go to the President for assent.

sections.title: Bills concerning county government
sections.simplified: Not all bills go through both Houses — but if a bill touches county government powers, elections, or finances, it is a 'county bill' and must be dealt with by both the National Assembly and the Senate following specific procedures.
examples: The national government introduces a bill that changes how county governors are elected. This directly relates to county executive elections — it is a special bill that must follow the specific bicameral procedure for county-related legislation.

A bill is introduced in Parliament and there is a dispute about whether it affects county governments. The Speakers of both Houses must jointly resolve the question before the bill proceeds — this prevents one House from unilaterally classifying bills to their advantage.

sections.title: Special Bills concerning county governments
sections.simplified: For the most important county-related bills, the National Assembly cannot simply override the Senate by a simple majority — they need two-thirds of all members. This gives the Senate stronger protection over matters that directly affect county government structures.
examples: The Senate passes a special bill on county assembly elections. The National Assembly disagrees with some provisions and wants to amend it. They must get at least two-thirds of all 350 Assembly members to vote for the amendment — a simple majority is not enough.

The National Assembly tries to veto a special county bill by a vote of 180 to 170. The veto fails — two-thirds of all members (at least 233 votes) are required to veto a special county bill passed by the Senate.

sections.title: Ordinary Bills concerning county governments
sections.simplified: When the two Houses of Parliament disagree on a bill affecting counties, they must try to resolve their differences through a joint mediation committee — not just let one House steamroll the other.
examples: The National Assembly passes a bill on county land management. The Senate passes it with significant amendments. The bill goes back to the National Assembly — which can accept the Senate's version, or if they reject it, both Houses must go to a mediation committee to agree on a single version.

After mediation fails to produce an agreed bill, the bill is defeated entirely. This is the constitutional outcome — if both Houses cannot agree through mediation, the bill dies and must be reintroduced from scratch.

sections.title: Mediation committees
sections.simplified: When Parliament's two Houses cannot agree on a bill, equal numbers of MPs and Senators sit together to find a compromise. If they cannot agree within 30 days, or if either House rejects the compromise, the bill dies completely — there is no way to force it through.
examples: A county finance bill is stuck between the two Houses with fundamentally different positions. A mediation committee of five MPs and five Senators works for 28 days to produce a compromise version. Both Houses must then vote to approve it — if either votes it down, the bill is finished.

A mediation committee cannot reach agreement within the 30-day deadline set by the Constitution. The bill automatically dies. The government must start the entire legislative process again if they still want the law — there are no shortcuts.

sections.title: Money Bills
sections.simplified: Bills about taxing people or spending public money can only start in the National Assembly — not in the Senate. This ensures that decisions about money ultimately come from the House directly elected by all Kenyans.
examples: A Senator introduces a bill proposing a new levy on goods imported into Kenya. This is a money bill — it cannot start in the Senate and must be reintroduced in the National Assembly.

The National Assembly proposes the annual Appropriation Bill — the law that authorises government spending for the year. This is a money Bill and correctly originates in the National Assembly. It cannot originate in the Senate.

sections.title: Presidential assent and referral
sections.simplified: The President has 14 days to sign a bill into law or send it back to Parliament with objections. Parliament can override presidential objections by a two-thirds majority. If the President does nothing within the deadline, the bill automatically becomes law.
examples: Parliament passes a healthcare bill and sends it to the President. The President sits on it for 20 days without acting. The bill automatically becomes law on day 14 — the President cannot kill a bill through inaction.

The President sends a controversial bill back to Parliament citing specific constitutional concerns. Parliament debates the concerns but votes to pass the bill unchanged with a two-thirds majority of both Houses. The President must now assent within 7 days — they cannot veto it a second time.

sections.title: Coming into force of laws
sections.simplified: Once a bill is signed, it must be published in the Gazette within 7 days and becomes law 14 days later — unless the law itself specifies a different start date. MPs cannot pass laws that immediately give themselves a financial benefit — those must wait until after the next election.
examples: Parliament passes a law raising MPs' salaries significantly. Even after presidential assent, this law cannot come into effect immediately — because it confers a financial benefit on MPs, it cannot take effect until after the next general election.
A new food safety law is published in the Gazette on 1st March. Unless the law specifies otherwise, it comes into force on 15th March — businesses must be compliant from that date.

sections.title: Powers, privileges and immunities
sections.simplified: What an MP says in Parliament — including criticisms and accusations — cannot be used against them in a court of law. This freedom of speech in Parliament exists to allow MPs to debate freely without fear of lawsuits for their contributions in the chamber.
examples: An MP makes a speech in Parliament accusing a minister of corruption, naming specific companies. The minister cannot sue the MP for defamation based on statements made in Parliament — parliamentary proceedings enjoy constitutional immunity.

An MP tries to hold a parliamentary committee hostage by threatening to leak information discussed in confidence. The committee's proceedings also enjoy certain constitutional protections — but this protection covers legitimate parliamentary business, not threats or abuse of the privilege.

sections.title: Public access and participation
sections.simplified: Parliament belongs to the people — its sessions are public by default. Citizens have the right to participate in lawmaking, not just watch. Parliament cannot routinely hold important business behind closed doors.
examples: A parliamentary committee meets in private to discuss a controversial bill on media regulation without public participation. This is unconstitutional — committee sittings must be public and must include opportunities for the public to contribute.

Residents of a county submit written memoranda to the Senate committee considering a healthcare bill that affects their county. The Constitution requires Parliament to facilitate public participation — these submissions must be considered, not ignored.

sections.title: Right to petition Parliament
sections.simplified: You have a constitutional right to formally ask Parliament to consider any matter — including demanding they pass a new law, change an existing one, or repeal a law that harms people. Parliament must have a clear process for receiving and considering these petitions.
examples: A disability rights organisation gathers 50,000 signatures and submits a petition to Parliament demanding the amendment of the traffic act to better protect pedestrians with disabilities. Parliament must receive and consider this petition — it is a constitutional right, not just a courtesy.

A single parent submits a petition to the National Assembly asking Parliament to review laws on child support to make enforcement faster and fairer. Parliament cannot simply ignore petitions — the Constitution requires a procedure for handling them and they must be considered.

sections.title: Official languages of Parliament
sections.simplified: Parliament conducts its business in Kiswahili, English, and Kenyan Sign Language — all three are official. This means Kenyan Sign Language has constitutional standing in Parliament, and laws must not create conflicts between different language versions.
examples: A Member of Parliament gives a speech in the National Assembly entirely in Kiswahili. This is fully constitutionally valid — Kiswahili is an official language of Parliament and MPs can use it freely.

Two versions of an Act of Parliament — one in English and one in Kiswahili — appear to have different meanings in a specific provision. The court looks at the version that was signed by the President to determine the official legal meaning.

sections.title: Quorum
sections.simplified: The National Assembly cannot conduct official business unless at least 50 of its 350 members are present. The Senate needs at least 15 members present. Votes and decisions taken without a quorum are constitutionally invalid.
examples: A contentious vote is called in the National Assembly late at night when only 30 members are present. Because the quorum of 50 is not met, any vote taken is unconstitutional — opposition members can challenge the validity of the result.
A bill is hurriedly passed by the Senate with only 10 Senators present. The quorum required is 15 — the bill was passed without a constitutional quorum and the passage is legally invalid.

sections.title: Voting in Parliament
sections.simplified: Parliament decides by majority of those who show up and vote — not majority of all members (unless the Constitution specifies otherwise for certain matters). The Speaker never votes. If it ends in a tie, the motion fails. MPs cannot vote on decisions where they personally stand to gain money.
examples: A bill passes with 175 MPs in favour and 175 against. Because it is a tie, the bill is defeated — the Speaker cannot cast a deciding vote, and there is no concept of the Speaker's vote as a tiebreaker in Parliament.

A procurement bill that would directly benefit a company an MP owns comes up for a vote. That MP is constitutionally prohibited from voting — voting on a question where you have a financial interest violates the Constitution.

sections.title: Decisions of Senate
sections.simplified: In the Senate, when voting on matters that affect counties, it is not individual senators who vote — it is county delegations as a whole. Each county gets one vote, not one vote per senator. This ensures no big county dominates small ones in county-related decisions.
examples: The Senate is voting on the allocation of national revenue among counties — a matter that directly affects counties. Nairobi's delegation of multiple senators casts just one vote on behalf of Nairobi county, same as Lamu's smaller delegation. Every county gets an equal voice.

A majority of individual senators support a county-related bill, but only 18 out of 47 county delegations as units vote in favour. The bill fails — county-affecting decisions require a majority of delegations (24 or more), not a majority of individual senators.

sections.title: Committees and Standing Orders
sections.simplified: Parliamentary committees do the detailed work of scrutinising bills, investigating government, and calling officials to account. Their work is legitimate even if there are vacancies in membership — Parliament doesn't have to be at full capacity to function.
examples: A Senate committee investigating county finance irregularities has three of its seven members absent due to illness. The committee can still legally proceed with its work — a vacancy in membership does not invalidate parliamentary committee proceedings.

The National Assembly establishes a Public Accounts Committee tasked with scrutinising government spending. Cabinet Secretaries must appear before it and answer questions — the committee has constitutional authority to conduct its oversight role.

sections.title: Power to call for evidence
sections.simplified: Parliamentary committees are powerful — they can summon anyone, including ministers, CEOs, and officials, to come and answer questions. Just like a court, they can enforce attendance and demand documents. Refusing to appear when summoned can be treated like contempt of court.
examples: A parliamentary committee investigating a government scandal summons the CEO of a parastatal to testify. The CEO refuses to appear. The committee can treat this as contempt and use its High Court equivalent powers to enforce his attendance — he cannot simply ignore the summons.

A National Assembly committee investigating irregular procurement demands all contracts and financial records from a ministry. The ministry must produce those documents — Parliament has the same power as the High Court to compel document production.

sections.title: Location of sittings of Parliament
sections.simplified: Parliament does not have to meet only in Nairobi. It can hold sessions anywhere in Kenya. After a general election, the President must summon the new Parliament within 30 days — there can be no prolonged delay in constituting a new Parliament.
examples: Parliament decides to hold a special session in Kisumu to hear public views on lake fishing legislation. This is constitutionally valid — Parliament can sit anywhere in Kenya, not just at Parliament Buildings in Nairobi.

After a general election, the President delays announcing the date for Parliament's first sitting for 40 days. This is unconstitutional — the first sitting must be scheduled within 30 days of the election.

sections.title: Parliamentary Service Commission
sections.simplified: The Parliamentary Service Commission runs the day-to-day administration of Parliament — managing staff, facilities, and budgets. It keeps Parliament functioning as an institution independently of the executive government.
examples: The government attempts to cut the parliamentary budget through the Treasury in a way that limits Parliament's ability to function effectively. The Parliamentary Service Commission prepares Parliament's own budget estimates — Parliament's financial independence is part of the constitutional separation of powers.

Parliament needs to hire additional research staff for its committees. The Parliamentary Service Commission handles all parliamentary staffing — it does not need approval from the Public Service Commission, protecting parliamentary independence.

sections.title: Clerks and staff of Parliament
sections.simplified: Each House has a Clerk who manages the administrative and procedural running of that House. The Clerk is accountable to Parliament through the Parliamentary Service Commission — not to any government ministry.
examples: The Cabinet Secretary for Public Service attempts to issue directives to the Clerks of Parliament regarding how they process bills. The Clerks are parliamentary service officials, not government employees — they do not fall under executive direction.

The Parliamentary Service Commission appoints a new Clerk of the National Assembly after the previous one retires. The National Assembly must also approve the appointment — this two-step process ensures the Clerk is both professionally qualified and has the confidence of the House.

sections.title: Principles of executive authority
sections.simplified: Government power comes from the people and must be used for the people. The executive — the President, Deputy President, and Cabinet — exists to serve Kenyans, not to enrich itself or exercise unchecked power.
examples: The President issues executive directives that benefit a small group of businesspeople at the expense of ordinary Kenyans. Executive authority must be exercised for the well-being of all Kenyans — selective use that harms the public interest can be challenged in court.

A Cabinet Secretary implements a policy without any public benefit but with significant personal gain for politically connected individuals. Since executive power must serve the people's well-being, such a policy can be challenged as an unconstitutional exercise of executive authority.

sections.title: The National Executive
sections.simplified: Kenya's executive government is the President, Deputy President, and Cabinet combined — not the President alone. The Cabinet must reflect Kenya's ethnic and regional diversity, meaning the President cannot fill it with people from only one region or community.
examples: A President appoints a Cabinet in which 18 out of 22 Cabinet Secretaries come from one ethnic community. This violates the constitutional requirement that the national executive reflect the regional and ethnic diversity of Kenya — it can be challenged in court.

The Deputy President and President together form just the top layer of executive power. The Cabinet Secretaries are also constitutionally part of the national executive — decisions that affect the country cannot be made by the President and Deputy President alone, bypassing Cabinet.

sections.title: Authority of the President
sections.simplified: The President is Kenya's most powerful official — but the Constitution defines exactly what that power is and what duties come with it. The President must protect human rights, respect diversity, and uphold the Constitution — not just command and rule.
examples: The President publicly endorses discrimination against a specific religious minority. This directly contradicts the constitutional duty to promote respect for diversity and ensure protection of human rights — such conduct can be cited in impeachment proceedings.

The President orders the military to violently suppress a peaceful protest. As Commander-in-Chief, the President leads the military, but all military actions must comply with the Constitution and respect human rights — unlawful orders can be refused and challenged.

sections.title: Functions of the President
sections.simplified: The President has specific, defined functions — from appointing Cabinet Secretaries to declaring war. Most major appointments and decisions require Parliamentary approval. The President cannot act unilaterally on the most significant matters of state.
examples: The President appoints a new Attorney-General and instructs them to begin work without seeking National Assembly approval. The Constitution requires the National Assembly to approve this appointment — the appointment is not valid until Parliament approves it.
Kenya is attacked by a neighbouring country. The President wants to declare war but cannot do so alone — the Constitution requires Parliament's approval before war can be officially declared.

sections.title: Power of mercy
sections.simplified: The President can pardon convicted criminals — but not on their own initiative. A specific advisory committee must recommend the pardon, and the President acts on their advice. This prevents pardons being used as political rewards.
examples: A prisoner serving a long sentence for a crime she committed when she was 17 petitions for a pardon based on her rehabilitation and good conduct in prison. The Advisory Committee reviews her case and may recommend a pardon — the President can then grant it based on that advice.

The President announces a personal decision to pardon a political ally convicted of corruption, without going through the Advisory Committee. This is unconstitutional — the power of mercy must be exercised on the advice of the committee, not as a personal presidential favour.

sections.title: Exercise of presidential powers during temporary incumbency
sections.simplified: When the President is temporarily absent, incapacitated, or there is a transitional period during elections, whoever is holding the presidency cannot make major permanent decisions. This prevents last-minute stacking of courts or rewarding of allies before a new President takes over.
examples: Kenya's President is in hospital and the Deputy President is acting President. During that period, the acting President cannot make new judicial appointments — such decisions must wait for the President's return or for a permanent new President to take office.

During the gap between an election result and the swearing-in of the new President, the outgoing President tries to appoint five new High Court judges. This is prohibited — the Constitution specifically bars such appointments during election transition periods.

sections.title: Decisions of the President
sections.simplified: Every official decision the President makes must be in writing with their signature and seal. Verbal presidential orders or undocumented decisions do not have constitutional force.
examples: A ministry begins implementing a policy claiming the President verbally approved it in a meeting. Without a written, signed and sealed presidential decision, that approval has no constitutional force — it is not an official presidential decision.
The President signs a written decree appointing a new Principal Secretary, with the official State seal affixed. This meets the constitutional requirement — the appointment is formally valid.

sections.title: Election of the President
sections.simplified: The President is chosen directly by all Kenyan voters — not by Parliament or any other body. Presidential elections happen on the same day as other general elections, every five years on the second Tuesday of August.
examples: A group proposes that future Presidents should be elected by Parliament rather than by direct popular vote, to save money. This would require a constitutional amendment — the direct election of the President by Kenyan citizens is a fundamental constitutional provision.

The general election is held on the second Tuesday of August. All presidential, parliamentary, county governor, and county assembly elections happen on the same day. Voting for President is a direct national vote by every registered Kenyan.

sections.title: Qualifications and disqualifications for election as President
sections.simplified: To run for President, you must be born Kenyan, meet MP eligibility standards, and demonstrate broad national support by collecting 2000 voter signatures from more than half of Kenya's 47 counties — proving your support is truly national, not just regional.
examples: A naturalised Kenyan citizen — born in Uganda but granted Kenyan citizenship — wants to run for President. He cannot — the Constitution requires presidential candidates to be citizens by birth, not by registration.

A politician wants to run for President but is still serving as a Principal Secretary. She must resign from her public office before contesting — active public officers are disqualified from standing for election as President.

sections.title: Procedure at presidential election
sections.simplified: Winning the presidency requires a double threshold — more than 50% of total votes, AND support spread across at least 24 of Kenya's 47 counties. A candidate who wins 60% of votes but only from two or three counties does not become President — national spread matters.
examples: Candidate A gets 51% of all votes cast but achieves 25% of the vote in only 20 counties. She has not met the constitutional threshold — she needs to hit 25% in at least 24 counties. A runoff election must be held between the top two candidates.
Only one candidate is formally nominated before the nomination deadline — no other candidate files. The sole candidate is automatically declared elected as President without a vote being held.

sections.title: Death before assuming office
sections.simplified: The Constitution has a clear succession plan if a tragedy occurs before inauguration. The Deputy President-elect steps up immediately as acting President and Kenya holds a new election within 60 days to choose a new President.
examples: The declared President-elect is killed in an accident before the inauguration date. The Deputy President-elect is immediately sworn in as acting President, and Kenya must hold a fresh presidential election within 60 days.

In a worst-case scenario where both the President-elect and Deputy President-elect die before inauguration, the Speaker of the National Assembly serves as acting President until a new election produces a winner.

sections.title: Questions as to validity of presidential election
sections.simplified: If you believe the presidential election was rigged, you have exactly 7 days to file a petition with the Supreme Court. The court then has 14 days to decide — no extensions. The Supreme Court's decision is final and cannot be appealed anywhere.
examples: A presidential candidate believes the IEBC manipulated results and wants to challenge the election. He must file his petition at the Supreme Court within 7 days of the result declaration — waiting even one day beyond that window means his case cannot be heard.

The Supreme Court hears a presidential election petition and rules the election invalid. The IEBC must organise a fresh presidential election within 60 days of that ruling — there is no way to delay or avoid a new election once the court rules the original one invalid.

sections.title: Assumption of office of President
sections.simplified: The inauguration of a new President happens on a specific day set by the Constitution, in public, before the Chief Justice. There is no room for a President to delay their own swearing-in, or to swear in a President in secret.
examples: Presidential results are declared on 15th August with no petition filed. The President-elect must be sworn in on the first Tuesday after 29th August — the date is constitutionally fixed, not discretionary.

A President-elect tries to hold a private swearing-in ceremony before the constitutionally set date. This is invalid — the oath must be taken in public, before the Chief Justice, on the constitutionally prescribed date.

sections.title: Term of office of President
sections.simplified: The President serves a five-year term and can only serve two terms total — whether consecutive or not. No President can change the Constitution to give themselves a third term, except through the full constitutional amendment process including a referendum.
examples: A President who served two full terms steps down and then ten years later wants to run again. The two-term limit under this article is absolute — regardless of how many years have passed, a person who has served two terms cannot hold the presidency again.

A sitting President promotes the idea of staying in power through a constitutional amendment that removes term limits. Such an amendment would require a national referendum — and even the attempt can be resisted as an affront to the Constitution's design.

sections.title: Protection from legal proceedings
sections.simplified: A sitting President cannot be taken to a Kenyan court — but this immunity expires the moment they leave office. And it does not protect them from international criminal charges — if charged with crimes against humanity or war crimes, the immunity does not apply.
examples: A businessman wants to sue the President for breach of contract over a deal made before the President took office. He must wait — civil proceedings against the sitting President for personal matters cannot proceed during the presidential term.

The International Criminal Court issues charges against a sitting Kenyan President for crimes against humanity. The presidential immunity does not extend to international criminal proceedings — the President cannot use it to block ICC prosecution.

sections.title: Removal of President on grounds of incapacity
sections.simplified: If a President is too ill to perform their duties, Parliament — through a medical process — can remove them. This is a health-based removal, not a punishment. It requires a very clear medical determination by qualified professionals.
examples: The President suffers a serious stroke that leaves them unable to communicate or make decisions, but the Deputy President refuses to take over. MPs can initiate this process — a quarter of all members start the motion and the rest of the constitutional process follows to ensure Kenya has a functioning head of state.

The medical tribunal appointed by the Chief Justice determines that the President is fully capable of performing their duties. The National Assembly must accept this finding — the process is designed around medical expertise, not political judgment.

sections.title: Removal of President by impeachment
sections.simplified: Impeachment is the political process for removing a President who has seriously failed their constitutional duties. It requires a very high threshold — two-thirds of the National Assembly must first pass the impeachment, and then two-thirds of the Senate must vote to uphold it.
examples: A President is caught on camera directly ordering police to shoot unarmed civilians. MPs present the evidence to the National Assembly — if a third support starting the process and two-thirds ultimately vote in favour, the case goes to the Senate for a full hearing and final determination.

The Senate investigates an impeachment charge but only 60% of senators vote to remove the President. Because the Constitution requires two-thirds of all senators, the President survives impeachment and returns to full duties — the high threshold is deliberately set to prevent political abuse of the process.

sections.title: Vacancy in the office of President
sections.simplified: If the President's office falls vacant, there is a clear and immediate succession plan. The Deputy President takes over — and if they cannot, the Speaker steps in temporarily while a new election is prepared. Kenya is never without a head of state.
examples: The President resigns from office by delivering a written resignation to the Speaker of the National Assembly. The Deputy President is immediately and automatically elevated to President for the remainder of the term — no election is needed unless the term had less than two and a half years remaining.

Both the President and Deputy President are removed from office simultaneously by impeachment. The Speaker of the National Assembly becomes acting President and a fresh presidential election must be held within 60 days.

sections.title: Functions of the Deputy President
sections.simplified: The Deputy President is the President's number two — not just a title holder. When the President is away or unable to act, the Deputy President steps in fully with all presidential authority. The President can also assign the Deputy President specific functions.
examples: The President travels abroad for an international summit. The Deputy President automatically acts as President during that period — they can sign documents, issue directives, and carry out presidential functions with full authority.

A President tries to maintain all presidential powers personally even while medically incapacitated in hospital, refusing to let the Deputy President act. The Constitution is clear — when the President is temporarily incapacitated, the Deputy President shall act as President.

sections.title: Election and swearing-in of Deputy President
sections.simplified: The Deputy President is not independently elected — they are the running mate chosen by the presidential candidate. When you vote for a presidential candidate, you are automatically also voting for their Deputy President pick.
examples: A voter assumes she can vote for one presidential candidate while voting for a different person's running mate as Deputy President. This is not how the Constitution works — the Deputy President is the running mate of whoever wins the presidency; they come as a pair.

A Deputy President who has served two full terms as Deputy President cannot serve as Deputy President again — the two-term limit on the deputy presidency is constitutionally enshrined just as it is for the presidency.

sections.title: Vacancy in the office of Deputy President
sections.simplified: If the Deputy Presidency falls vacant, the President must quickly nominate a replacement, and Parliament must confirm or reject the nominee within 60 days. The country cannot go without a Deputy President for an extended period.
examples: The Deputy President resigns to run for a different political position. The President has 14 days to nominate a new Deputy President. The National Assembly then has 60 days to vote on whether to approve the nomination.

The President takes three months to nominate a new Deputy President after the previous one died in office. This delay is unconstitutional — the nomination must happen within 14 days of the vacancy arising.

sections.title: Removal of Deputy President
sections.simplified: The Deputy President can be removed from office through the same processes as the President — either on health grounds through a medical tribunal or through impeachment by Parliament. The same high thresholds apply.
examples: The Deputy President is accused of orchestrating a bribery scheme. A third of National Assembly members can initiate impeachment proceedings using exactly the same process as for presidential impeachment — the constitutional protections and thresholds are identical.

The Deputy President develops a serious health condition that makes performing their functions impossible. The same medical tribunal process used for the President can be initiated to formally remove the Deputy President on grounds of incapacity.

sections.title: Remuneration and benefits of President and Deputy President
sections.simplified: The President and Deputy President's pay is guaranteed by the Constitution and paid directly from the national treasury — Parliament cannot cut their salaries to pressure them politically. Their retirement benefits are also protected for life after leaving office.
examples: Parliament, in a dispute with the President, votes to cut the presidential salary to zero as a pressure tactic. This is unconstitutional — the Constitution guarantees the President's remuneration and it cannot be varied to their disadvantage while in office.

A former President's retirement benefits are reduced by a subsequent administration unhappy with the previous President's record. This is unconstitutional — retirement benefits of former Presidents are protected and cannot be reduced during their lifetime.

sections.title: Cabinet
sections.simplified: The Cabinet has a minimum of 14 and maximum of 22 Cabinet Secretaries — limiting its size to prevent bloated government. Every Cabinet Secretary must be approved by the National Assembly, and they cannot also be a sitting MP — you cannot be a Cabinet Secretary and an MP at the same time.
examples: The President wants to nominate an MP she trusts as a Cabinet Secretary. The MP must first resign from Parliament — a Cabinet Secretary cannot simultaneously hold a seat in Parliament, and the Constitution is explicit about this.
The President nominates 25 Cabinet Secretaries. Even if all are well qualified, this exceeds the constitutional maximum of 22 — the nominations that push beyond the cap are constitutionally invalid.

sections.title: Decisions, responsibility and accountability of the Cabinet
sections.simplified: Cabinet is collectively responsible for its decisions, and each Cabinet Secretary is personally accountable for their ministry. They cannot hide from Parliament — they must appear before parliamentary committees and answer questions about how they are running their ministries.
examples: The Health Cabinet Secretary refuses to appear before the National Assembly's Health Committee, claiming the session is not convenient. The Constitution requires Cabinet Secretaries to attend when required by Parliament — refusal is a constitutional violation.

A Cabinet Secretary claims in public that a decision was made personally by the President and the Cabinet cannot be blamed. The Constitution requires Cabinet decisions to be collective — the entire Cabinet is responsible for decisions made by the Cabinet collectively.

sections.title: Secretary to the Cabinet
sections.simplified: The Secretary to the Cabinet is the senior civil servant who runs the administrative machinery of Cabinet — managing meetings, keeping minutes, and conveying decisions. They are a public service position, not a political appointee in the partisan sense.
examples: The Secretary to the Cabinet secretly destroys Cabinet minutes from a controversial meeting. This is a serious breach — Cabinet decisions must be documented in writing and kept officially. Destroying records is unconstitutional and potentially criminal.

The President fires the Secretary to the Cabinet overnight without any stated reason. While the President can dismiss the Secretary, even dismissal from public office must follow due process principles — the Secretary can challenge an arbitrary dismissal.

sections.title: Principal Secretaries
sections.simplified: Principal Secretaries are the top civil servants running individual government departments. Their appointment goes through the Public Service Commission and Parliament — they are not purely presidential personal picks.
examples: The President wants to appoint a personal ally as Principal Secretary in the Treasury without going through the Public Service Commission. This is unconstitutional — Principal Secretary nominees must come from persons recommended by the Public Service Commission.

The National Assembly rejects a President's nominated Principal Secretary during the vetting process. The nomination fails — National Assembly approval is required, and Parliament's rejection means the person cannot be appointed.

sections.title: Attorney-General
sections.simplified: The Attorney-General is Kenya's top government lawyer — but their primary duty is to the law and public interest, not personal loyalty to the President. They advise government and represent it in court, but must defend the rule of law even when it is inconvenient for the government.
examples: A President pressures the Attorney-General to give a legal opinion that an unconstitutional policy is lawful. The Attorney-General's constitutional duty is to uphold the rule of law and public interest — they must give honest legal advice, even when it is not what the President wants to hear.

The government is sued by a public interest organisation over a policy decision. The Attorney-General represents the national government in that court case — defending the government's legal position before the court.

sections.title: Director of Public Prosecutions
sections.simplified: The DPP decides who gets prosecuted for crimes in Kenya — independently and without political interference. No President, minister, or court can tell the DPP who to prosecute or not. And to protect that independence, the DPP serves only one 8-year term and cannot be reappointed.
examples: A minister calls the DPP and instructs them to drop criminal charges against a political ally. The DPP is constitutionally independent — they are not subject to direction or control by any person, and must make prosecution decisions based on the law and public interest alone.

The DPP directs the Inspector-General of Police to investigate a senior government official for financial crimes. The police must comply — the DPP has constitutional authority to direct investigations, and police compliance is not optional.

sections.title: Removal and resignation of Director of Public Prosecutions
sections.simplified: The DPP is protected from politically motivated removal — they can only be removed through a formal process involving specific grounds, a petition, and an independent tribunal. A government unhappy with prosecutorial decisions cannot simply fire the DPP.
examples: The government tries to remove the DPP after he charges several Cabinet Secretaries with corruption. Removal requires a formal petition on specific constitutional grounds, a Public Service Commission review, and a presidential tribunal — a politically motivated removal attempt would fail at multiple steps.

The DPP is found to have issued false academic credentials during their appointment process. Non-compliance with Chapter 6 integrity requirements is a valid ground for removal — a petition can be legitimately filed and the process followed.

sections.title: Judicial authority
sections.simplified: Courts exist to deliver justice to everyone — rich or poor, powerful or ordinary. Justice must be quick, accessible, and not strangled by unnecessary procedural rules. Courts must also consider alternative ways of resolving disputes like mediation before insisting on a full trial.
examples: A court dismisses a valid legal case because the claimant used the wrong form when filing their papers, even though the substance of the case is clear. The Constitution requires courts not to be unreasonably restricted by procedural technicalities — a technical error alone should not defeat a legitimate claim.

Two neighbours have a land boundary dispute. Before taking it to court, they try community mediation. The Constitution encourages this — alternative dispute resolution is explicitly promoted as a legitimate way to resolve conflicts.

sections.title: Independence of the Judiciary
sections.simplified: No President, politician, or government can tell a judge how to decide a case. The judiciary answers only to the law and the Constitution. Judges' positions are also protected — you cannot fire a judge or cut their pay to punish them for inconvenient rulings.
examples: A powerful Cabinet Secretary calls a judge and suggests how a case involving his ministry should be decided. This is unconstitutional interference with judicial independence — the judge must report the call and decide the case purely on its legal merits.

Parliament passes a law cutting judges' salaries by 50% after judges issued several rulings against the government. This is unconstitutional — remuneration and benefits payable to judges cannot be varied to their disadvantage.

sections.title: Judicial offices and officers
sections.simplified: Kenya's judicial system is structured with the Chief Justice at the top, followed by the Deputy CJ, then judges of various courts, magistrates, and judicial staff. The Chief Registrar manages the administrative running of the judiciary.
examples: A petitioner wants to understand who runs Kenya's court system administratively. The Chief Registrar is the chief administrator of the judiciary — handling administrative and financial matters so judges can focus on deciding cases.

A new court is being established and needs registrars to manage its administrative functions. The Judicial Service Commission has the authority to create registrar offices as needed — it does not need parliamentary approval for administrative judicial offices.

sections.title: System of courts
sections.simplified: Kenya has a clear court hierarchy. At the top is the Supreme Court, then the Court of Appeal, then the High Court. For specialised matters, there are the Employment and Labour Relations Court and the Environment and Land Court, both with High Court status.
examples: A worker is dismissed unfairly and wants to sue her employer. She should go to the Employment and Labour Relations Court — a specialised court with the same status as the High Court, specifically set up for employment disputes.

A community has a major dispute over land title and compensation. The Environment and Land Court is the specialised court for land disputes — filing in the regular High Court on a land matter may result in the case being transferred.

sections.title: Supreme Court
sections.simplified: The Supreme Court is Kenya's highest court. It is the only court that can hear disputes about presidential elections, and its decisions bind every other court in the country. You can appeal to the Supreme Court from the Court of Appeal, especially on constitutional matters.
examples: A presidential election petition is filed in the High Court by mistake. The Constitution gives the Supreme Court exclusive jurisdiction over presidential election disputes — the petition must be in the Supreme Court, not any other court.

A Court of Appeal ruling on a constitutional matter creates confusion across other courts. A Supreme Court ruling on the same constitutional question settles it definitively — all courts, including the Court of Appeal, must follow Supreme Court decisions.

sections.title: Court of Appeal
sections.simplified: The Court of Appeal is the second highest court in Kenya. If you lose a case in the High Court and believe the judge made a legal error, you can appeal to the Court of Appeal to have the decision reviewed.
examples: A person loses their property rights case in the High Court and believes the judge misapplied the law. They can appeal to the Court of Appeal, which will review whether the High Court's legal reasoning was correct — not just whether you liked the outcome.

A criminal convicted by the High Court believes the sentence was excessive or the conviction was wrong. They have the right to appeal to the Court of Appeal — this is a fundamental part of the right to a fair trial protected in the Bill of Rights.

sections.title: High Court
sections.simplified: The High Court is the most powerful trial court in Kenya. It can hear any case — criminal or civil — without limits on the value or nature of the claim. It is also the primary court for enforcing your constitutional rights and for interpreting the Constitution when there is a dispute.
examples: A person believes their right to privacy has been violated by a government agency. They go directly to the High Court — it has jurisdiction to determine whether a Bill of Rights right has been violated and can order remedies.

A magistrate court delivers what appears to be a very unfair ruling in a case. The High Court has supervisory jurisdiction over subordinate courts — it can review the magistrate's decision to ensure it was legally sound.

sections.title: Appointment of Chief Justice, Deputy Chief Justice and other judges
sections.simplified: Judges do not get their positions through political patronage — they go through a merit-based process managed by the Judicial Service Commission. The most senior judges (Chief Justice and Deputy CJ) also require Parliamentary approval, giving citizens a further check on who leads the judiciary.
examples: The President wants to appoint a personal friend as Chief Justice without going through the Judicial Service Commission process. This is unconstitutional — the Chief Justice appointment must follow the JSC's recommendation and receive National Assembly approval.

A person with only 5 years of legal practice applies to be appointed as a High Court judge. The Constitution requires at least 10 years of relevant experience — the candidate is not qualified regardless of other factors.

sections.title: Tenure of office of the Chief Justice and other judges
sections.simplified: Judges have guaranteed tenure until age 70, protecting them from political pressure. The Chief Justice has an additional limit of 10 years in the top role. A judge cannot be forced out just because the government dislikes their rulings.
examples: The government tries to pass a law forcing all judges over 65 to retire. The Constitution sets retirement at 70 — judges can choose to retire at 65 but cannot be compelled to until 70. The law would be unconstitutional.

A Chief Justice is appointed at age 58. Even if they would not reach 70 for 12 more years, their maximum term as Chief Justice is 10 years — they must step down from the CJ role after 10 years, though they can continue as a regular Supreme Court judge.

sections.title: Removal from office
sections.simplified: Judges can only be removed through a rigorous independent process — not because a politician dislikes a ruling. A tribunal investigates, makes binding recommendations, and the judge can appeal to the Supreme Court. This multi-step process protects judicial independence while still holding judges accountable.
examples: A High Court judge is caught accepting a bribe to alter a court ruling. This is gross misconduct — a petition can be filed with the Judicial Service Commission, which initiates the removal process. The tribunal will investigate and make binding recommendations.

The government tries to fast-track the removal of a judge who ruled against it in a high-profile case, alleging incompetence. The constitutional process requires a JSC investigation before a petition reaches the President — a politically motivated process will be exposed and challenged.

sections.title: Subordinate courts
sections.simplified: Below the High Court is the magistrate court — the most accessible court for most Kenyans, handling everyday criminal and civil cases. There are also specialised courts for Muslim family law (Kadhis' courts) and military discipline (Courts Martial).
examples: A resident of a rural town is accused of theft and the case is prosecuted by police. The case will be heard in the local magistrate's court — subordinate courts handle the vast majority of Kenya's criminal and civil matters at the grassroots level.

A Muslim couple in Mombasa disputes how their marriage property should be divided after divorce, both agreeing to resolve the matter under Islamic law. The Kadhis' court has jurisdiction to hear Muslim personal status matters where all parties are Muslim and consent to the court's jurisdiction.

sections.title: Kadhis’ courts
sections.simplified: Kadhis' courts handle Muslim family law matters — marriage, divorce, and inheritance — but only when all the people involved are Muslim and agree to use the court. A Muslim cannot be forced to use a Kadhi's court, and non-Muslims cannot be brought before one.
examples: A Muslim man wants his divorce processed by a Kadhi's court, but his wife prefers to use the civil courts. Because she does not consent to the Kadhi's court jurisdiction, the matter must be handled by the regular civil courts — the Kadhi's court requires all parties to consent.

A non-Muslim woman is in a dispute with a Muslim business partner about inheritance. This is a commercial matter, not a personal status issue, and it involves a non-Muslim — the Kadhi's court has no jurisdiction. The case goes to the regular civil courts.

sections.title: Establishment of the Judicial Service Commission
sections.simplified: The Judicial Service Commission manages Kenya's judiciary — appointing judges, handling complaints against them, and disciplining or removing them. Its composition brings together judicial officers, advocates, and members of the public to ensure it is not controlled by any single group.
examples: A member of the public believes a magistrate in their town is consistently biased and takes bribes. They can file a complaint with the Judicial Service Commission, which has the authority to investigate judicial officers and take disciplinary action.

A new batch of High Court judges needs to be appointed. The JSC manages the entire competitive process — advertising the positions, interviewing candidates, evaluating their qualifications and character, and making recommendations to the President.

sections.title: Functions of the Judicial Service Commission
sections.simplified: The JSC is both the guardian of judicial independence and the body responsible for judicial accountability. It balances ensuring judges are free from political interference while also ensuring they perform their duties properly and are held accountable when they fail.
examples: The Chief Justice notices that several magistrate courts have huge backlogs of cases because magistrates are not being productive. The JSC can investigate, provide training, and take disciplinary action against magistrates who are not performing their duties.

The government proposes a policy that would place the judiciary under executive oversight for budgeting purposes. The JSC can advise government that this would undermine judicial independence — one of its core constitutional functions.

sections.title: Judiciary Fund
sections.simplified: The judiciary has its own separate fund — it does not depend on ministry allocation for its budget. The Chief Registrar manages the money, and Parliament approves the annual budget. This financial independence supports judicial independence.
examples: The executive government threatens to withhold judiciary funding unless certain cases are decided in their favour. The Judiciary Fund is constitutionally established and its funding flows directly from the Consolidated Fund upon parliamentary approval — the executive cannot unilaterally withhold it.

The Chief Registrar prepares the judiciary's budget for the next financial year and submits it to the National Assembly. Parliament must consider and approve it — but cannot reduce it to a level that prevents the judiciary from functioning effectively.

chapters.title: DEVOLVED GOVERNMENT
chapters.description: How county and national government share power

sections.title: Objects of devolution
sections.simplified: Devolution is not just about having 47 counties — it is about bringing power closer to you. The Constitution says decisions that affect your community should be made by your community, not by distant politicians in Nairobi. Devolution also ensures no single area monopolises national resources.
examples: Residents of a county argue that all major infrastructure decisions for their area should be made in Nairobi. Devolution was designed precisely to give communities the power to make decisions affecting them locally — county governments have constitutional authority over many such decisions.

A wealthy county with a large tax base gets the same equitable share allocation as a poor county, despite contributing more. The objects of devolution include equitable sharing of national and local resources throughout Kenya — poorer counties must receive fair support to develop.

sections.title: Principles of devolved government
sections.simplified: County governments must operate democratically with checks between the executive and assembly. Counties must have their own reliable money — not be financially starved by the national government. And the two-thirds gender rule applies at county level too.
examples: A county governor repeatedly ignores the county assembly and implements programmes without assembly approval. This violates the principle of separation of powers that must apply in counties — the assembly has legislative oversight authority that the governor cannot bypass.
A county assembly is elected with 70% male members. This exceeds the two-thirds gender limit — the same constitutional rule that applies in national Parliament applies in county assemblies.

sections.title: County governments
sections.simplified: Each of Kenya's 47 counties has its own elected government — an assembly (like a mini-Parliament) and an executive headed by the governor. County governments must also bring services closer to people — they cannot centralise everything at the county headquarters.
examples: A county government runs all healthcare services from the county headquarters only, making it impossible for people in remote sub-counties to access services. The Constitution requires counties to decentralise service delivery to the extent practicable — this centralised approach can be challenged.

Residents in a ward of a large county have no access to any county government services. The county government's constitutional duty includes decentralising services — having sub-county and ward-level offices and service delivery points is part of fulfilling this duty.

sections.title: Membership of county assembly
sections.simplified: Your county assembly has three layers of membership — your directly elected ward representative, additional special seat members to balance the gender ratio, and members representing persons with disabilities and youth. Every ward in Kenya has a directly elected MCA.
examples: Voters in Nyali Ward elect their Member of the County Assembly directly. This ward MCA is their primary representative at county level — bringing local issues to the county assembly and voting on county legislation and the county budget.

After ward elections in a county, the assembly is 72% male. Additional female special seat members must be nominated by political parties proportionally to bring the gender ratio within the two-thirds rule — this is constitutionally mandatory, not optional.

sections.title: Speaker of a county assembly
sections.simplified: County assembly speakers are elected by the assembly from people outside the assembly — just like Parliament's Speaker. This ensures the presiding officer is neutral and not simultaneously voting on legislation.
examples: A sitting Member of a County Assembly wants to be elected as Speaker of that same assembly without first resigning their ward seat. The Constitution requires the Speaker to be someone who is not a member of the assembly — the MCA must resign their ward seat before being eligible.

The Speaker of a county assembly is absent when a vote is called. Another member of the assembly is elected to preside for that session — county assembly proceedings can continue even when the Speaker is not present.

sections.title: County executive committees
sections.simplified: The county governor is not a one-person ruler — they lead an executive committee that includes a deputy and other appointed members. Just like at national level, major executive decisions are collective, and the assembly must approve key appointments.
examples: A county governor makes all executive decisions personally without convening or consulting the county executive committee. The Constitution vests executive authority in the committee, not the governor alone — bypassing the committee for major decisions is unconstitutional.

The governor appoints a county executive committee member without seeking county assembly approval. The Constitution requires assembly approval for committee appointments — the appointment is not valid until approved.

sections.title: Election of county governor and deputy county governor
sections.simplified: Governors are elected directly by county voters — you vote for your governor personally, not through representatives. The governor and deputy run together as a pair. And just like the President, governors are limited to two terms.
examples: A governor who has served two full terms wants to run for governor again in a different county. The Constitution's two-term limit on county governors is not county-specific — a person cannot hold office as governor for more than two terms total, regardless of which county.

A county governor wins a second term. Their running mate is automatically declared elected deputy governor without a separate election — voters chose the governor, and the deputy comes as part of the package, just as in a presidential election.

sections.title: Removal of a county governor
sections.simplified: A county governor can be removed — but only through a formal legal process based on specific serious grounds. Removing a governor for personal or political reasons without following the proper process is unconstitutional.
examples: A governor is caught misappropriating county funds for personal use. This constitutes abuse of office — the county assembly can initiate removal proceedings under the legislated procedure, leading to the governor's removal if the process is successfully completed.

County assembly members try to remove a governor because they dislike his development priorities. Disagreement over policy is not a constitutional ground for removal — only the specific grounds listed in this article can support a removal process.

sections.title: Vacancy in the office of county governor
sections.simplified: If a governor's seat falls vacant, the deputy immediately steps up — just like national government succession. If both governor and deputy are gone simultaneously, the county assembly speaker temporarily takes over while a new election is organised.
examples: A county governor is convicted of fraud and sentenced to 18 months in prison. Because the conviction carries imprisonment of more than 12 months, the governor's office becomes vacant — the deputy automatically becomes governor.

Both the governor and deputy governor of a county are killed in an accident. The speaker of the county assembly assumes the governorship temporarily, and a fresh county gubernatorial election must be held within 60 days.

sections.title: Functions of county executive committees
sections.simplified: The county executive committee implements laws — both county-made laws and national laws that require local implementation. They also manage county administration and must regularly report to the county assembly on what they are doing with public resources.
examples: A county executive committee refuses to present its quarterly report to the county assembly, claiming the assembly has no right to that information. The Constitution explicitly requires the executive committee to provide the assembly with full and regular reports — this refusal is unconstitutional.

The national government passes a law requiring counties to implement a new maternal health programme. The county executive committee must implement this national legislation within its county — they cannot simply ignore national laws because they prefer their own programmes.

sections.title: Urban areas and cities
sections.simplified: Cities and towns need special governance structures to deal with their unique challenges — Parliament must pass laws defining what qualifies as a city or town and how residents can participate in governing their urban area.
examples: Residents of a rapidly growing town want to know who is responsible for their urban planning and waste management. The Constitution requires national legislation to establish the governance structure for urban areas — this clarity helps residents know who to hold accountable.

A large urban area gets classified as a city under national legislation. The residents of that city must be given meaningful ways to participate in decisions affecting their city — including urban planning, public transport, and local services.

sections.title: Legislative authority of county assemblies
sections.simplified: County assemblies make laws — real laws, with legal force in the county. They also oversee the governor's executive committee, just as Parliament oversees the national executive. MCAs are not just rubber stamps; they have genuine legislative and oversight power.
examples: A county assembly passes a by-law requiring all businesses in the county to register locally and pay a county business levy. This is valid county legislation — the assembly has authority to make laws necessary for county government functions.

The county assembly summons the governor's economic advisor to explain a controversial contract awarded to a foreign company. This is the assembly exercising its constitutional oversight function — county executives must account to the assembly for their decisions.

sections.title: Respective functions and powers of national and county governments
sections.simplified: The Constitution clearly divides responsibilities between the national government and counties. Some functions are purely national (like defence), some are purely county (like local markets), and some are shared. When it is not clear who is responsible, the national government has the residual power.
examples: A county government decides to establish its own foreign policy and begin negotiating trade deals with foreign countries. Foreign affairs is a national government function — counties have no constitutional authority in that area.

A dispute arises over who is responsible for a public hospital — the national or county government. County health services (county hospitals and clinics) are a county function, but national referral hospitals remain a national function. The Fourth Schedule resolves such disputes.

sections.title: Transfer of functions and powers between levels of government
sections.simplified: National and county governments can agree to transfer functions between themselves — but the money to perform those functions must move with them. A county cannot be given more responsibilities without also receiving the resources needed to carry them out.
examples: The national government agrees to transfer road maintenance responsibilities for certain county roads to a county government. The county must also receive the allocated budget for that road maintenance — the Constitution requires resources to follow functions.

A county asks to take over a function currently managed by the national government. The transfer is possible through a formal agreement — but if the law governing that function prohibits transfer, it cannot happen regardless of what both governments agree.

sections.title: Boundaries of counties
sections.simplified: County boundaries cannot be changed by politicians on a whim. Any change requires an independent commission's recommendation plus a super-majority in both Houses of Parliament. This protects communities from having their county territories arbitrarily redrawn.
examples: The national government announces a plan to redraw boundaries between two counties by presidential decree. This is unconstitutional — county boundary changes require an independent commission recommendation and a super-majority vote in both Houses of Parliament.

Residents of a border area between two counties petition for their area to be moved from one county to another. The process requires Parliament to set up an independent commission, and both Houses must approve the change by two-thirds majorities.

sections.title: Cooperation between national and county governments
sections.simplified: The national government and county governments must work as partners — not as a boss and subordinate. The national government cannot ignore or undermine county governments, and counties must equally work within the national framework. Disputes must be resolved through negotiation, not confrontation.
examples: The national government begins implementing a major water project in a county without informing or consulting the county government. The Constitution requires governments to consult each other — implementing major projects in a county without county government involvement is unconstitutional.

A county government and the national government set up a joint committee to coordinate the rollout of a universal health coverage programme in the county. This is exactly the type of cooperative structure the Constitution envisions for shared functions.

sections.title: Support for county governments
sections.simplified: The national government must support counties so they can do their jobs properly. But if a county completely fails to function or manage money responsibly, the national government can step in — though this must follow a specific process with Senate oversight.
examples: A county government repeatedly fails to pay health workers' salaries and medical facilities run out of supplies. The national government can intervene to ensure services continue — but the Senate must be involved and the intervention must be proportionate and aimed at helping the county recover.
