A county requests technical support from the national government for implementing a complex infrastructure project. The Constitution requires national government to support county governments — providing technical assistance is a legitimate and expected form of support.

sections.title: Conflict of laws
sections.simplified: When national and county laws conflict, there are clear rules for who wins. National law wins for big-picture national matters. County law wins for local matters where national uniformity is not needed. Courts must always try to find an interpretation that avoids a conflict.
examples: A county passes a by-law setting its own standards for food imports that differ from national standards. Because consumer protection and food standards require national uniformity to be effective, the national law prevails over the county by-law.

A county passes a law about management of local markets that differs slightly from national market management guidelines. Because this is a local matter where national uniformity is not required, the county law may prevail over the national guidelines.

sections.title: Suspension of a county government
sections.simplified: The national government cannot simply dissolve a county government it dislikes. Suspension requires an independent investigation, presidential satisfaction that the allegations are justified, and Senate approval. Even then, it lasts no more than 90 days.
examples: The President suspends a county government that has been vocal in opposing national policies, citing 'exceptional circumstances.' Without an independent commission's investigation and Senate approval, the suspension is unconstitutional — political opposition is not a ground for suspension.

A county government completely collapses due to internal factional fighting that disrupts service delivery. The national government, after an independent commission investigates and the Senate approves, can suspend the county for up to 90 days while stabilising the situation.

sections.title: Qualifications for election as member of county assembly
sections.simplified: To be a Member of the County Assembly, you need 500 ward voter signatures if running as an independent — much lower than Parliament — reflecting that county assembly members represent smaller, local communities.
examples: A young community leader in a ward wants to run as an MCA. If running as an independent, she needs signatures from at least 500 registered voters in her ward. This is a much more achievable threshold than national Parliament — it is designed to keep county assembly accessible to community-level candidates.

A ward officer working in the county government wants to contest the county assembly seat for his ward. He must resign from the county government position first — public officers are disqualified from standing for election while in office.

sections.title: Vacation of office of member of county assembly
sections.simplified: MCAs can lose their seats for the same reasons as MPs — including abandoning their party if they were elected on that party's ticket. If you were elected as an independent MCA and then join a political party, you immediately lose your seat.
examples: An MCA elected as a party candidate publicly resigns from that party and declares support for a rival. Under the Constitution, leaving the party you were elected on causes you to lose your county assembly seat — the vacancy must be filled.

An MCA misses 10 consecutive sittings without seeking the Speaker's permission and provides no acceptable reason. The seat becomes vacant — county assembly members have a constitutional obligation to attend and participate in assembly business.

sections.title: County assembly power to summon witnesses
sections.simplified: County assembly committees have the same power to summon witnesses as the High Court. A county official, contractor, or any person cannot simply refuse to appear before a county assembly committee that has summoned them.
examples: A county assembly committee investigating irregular tender awards summons a private contractor to explain how they got the contract. The contractor cannot simply ignore the summons — the county assembly has High Court-equivalent powers to compel attendance.

A county executive committee member refuses to produce financial records requested by the county assembly. The assembly can use its power to compel production of documents — just as a High Court can order document disclosure.

sections.title: Public participation and county assembly powers, privileges and immunities
sections.simplified: County assembly proceedings must be open to the public — MCAs cannot legislate in secret. Citizens have the constitutional right to participate in their county assembly's work, not just to watch from the gallery.
examples: A county assembly holds budget discussions in a private session without notifying or inviting the public. This is unconstitutional — county assembly business must be conducted openly and the public must be able to participate, especially on major matters like the budget.

Residents of a county want to submit their views on a proposed county education policy before the assembly passes it into law. They have a constitutional right to participate — the assembly must create mechanisms for public input into legislation.

sections.title: County assembly gender balance and diversity
sections.simplified: The two-thirds gender rule applies everywhere in county government — both in the assembly and in the executive committee. County governments must also reflect the ethnic and cultural diversity within their county, not be dominated by one community.
examples: A county governor appoints an executive committee in which 9 of 10 members are from the same ethnic community as the governor. The Constitution requires the committee to reflect the county's community and cultural diversity — this composition can be legally challenged.

After county assembly elections, the gender balance is corrected through special seat nominations to ensure not more than two-thirds of members are of the same gender. This is the constitutional mechanism — the two-thirds rule must be achieved, not just attempted.

sections.title: County government during transition
sections.simplified: When a county goes to election, the old county executive does not immediately vacate — they stay on in a caretaker capacity to keep the county functioning until a new government is formed after the election.
examples: On election day in a county, the governor and executive committee continue to perform their administrative functions even as voting happens. They are in caretaker mode — they can run day-to-day operations but cannot make major new decisions during this transitional period.

A county assembly is dissolved before a general election but essential county services like hospitals and schools must continue operating. The existing executive committee maintains administrative functions — essential services do not shut down during election transitions.

sections.title: Publication of county legislation
sections.simplified: A county law only becomes effective and binding once it is published in the official Gazette. A county assembly can pass a law, but it has no force until gazetted — residents cannot be expected to comply with laws that are not publicly published.
examples: A county governor begins enforcing a new county by-law that the assembly passed but that has not yet been gazetted. Enforcement of an un-gazetted law is unlawful — the by-law has no legal effect until it is published in the Gazette.

A business owner is fined for violating a county regulation. She checks and finds the regulation has never been published in the Gazette. The fine is invalid — county legislation that has not been gazetted has no legal effect.

sections.title: Legislation on Chapter
sections.simplified: Parliament must pass detailed laws to make the devolution chapter fully operational. Without these enabling laws, many provisions of devolution remain aspirational — Parliament has a constitutional duty to make devolution real and workable.
examples: Disputes arise about how to govern Nairobi City given that it is both a county and the national capital. Parliament is constitutionally required to pass legislation specifically for the governance of the capital city — citizens can push for this legislation if Parliament delays.

A county government and national government disagree about which functions have been transferred to the county and what resources should follow. Parliament must enact clear legislation on the transfer of functions and accompanying resources — ambiguity on this point is a legislative failure.

chapters.title: PUBLIC FINANCE
chapters.description: How public money should be raised and used

sections.title: Principles of public finance
sections.simplified: Kenya's public money must be managed openly, fairly, and responsibly. Tax burdens must be shared fairly — the rich should pay more than the poor. Revenue must be distributed equitably between national and county levels. And future generations should not be burdened by today's reckless borrowing.
examples: The national government borrows excessively to fund projects with no clear repayment plan, saddling future Kenyans with unmanageable debt. The Constitution requires that burdens of public borrowing be shared equitably between present and future generations — this reckless borrowing violates that principle.

A county government spends public money without publishing expenditure reports or allowing public scrutiny. The Constitution requires openness, accountability, and public participation in financial matters — closed financial management at county level is unconstitutional.

sections.title: Equitable sharing of national revenue
sections.simplified: The money Kenya collects in taxes and other revenue must be shared fairly between the national government and the 47 counties. Counties can also receive extra allocations — with or without conditions attached — beyond their basic share.
examples: The national government collects KES 2 trillion in revenue. A constitutionally defined equitable share — at least 15% — must be transferred to the county governments to fund their services. The national government cannot retain all of it.

A county that hosts a major national park is given an additional unconditional allocation to compensate communities for conservation restrictions on their land. This additional allocation on top of the equitable share is constitutionally permitted.

sections.title: Equitable share and other financial laws
sections.simplified: At minimum, 15% of all nationally collected revenue must go to counties — this is a constitutional floor, not a ceiling. The actual allocation is decided based on multiple criteria including counties' needs, capacity, and development gaps.
examples: Parliament passes a Division of Revenue Bill allocating only 12% of national revenue to counties. This is unconstitutional — the Constitution guarantees counties at least 15%, and Parliament cannot pass a bill that violates this floor.

A sparsely populated arid county with few economic resources receives a larger share than a more developed county. The allocation criteria include economic disparities and developmental needs — poorer, less developed counties are entitled to larger allocations to help them catch up.

sections.title: Equalisation Fund
sections.simplified: Half a percent of all Kenya's tax revenue is set aside specifically to bring basic services to marginalised areas — arid regions, remote communities, historically neglected parts of the country. This is targeted development funding, constitutionally guaranteed.
examples: A remote community in Mandera has no health facility and no clean water supply. The Equalisation Fund is constitutionally designed to fund exactly this — water and health facilities in marginalised areas to bring them to the level the rest of Kenya enjoys.

The government uses Equalisation Fund money to build a road in Nairobi rather than in marginalised areas. This is a constitutional violation — the Fund can only be used in marginalised areas and specifically for basic services like water, roads, health, and electricity.

sections.title: Consultation on financial legislation affecting counties
sections.simplified: Before Parliament votes on any law about money and counties, the Commission on Revenue Allocation must be given the chance to review it and make recommendations. Parliament must then actually consider those recommendations before voting — they cannot be ignored.
examples: Parliament rushes through a Division of Revenue Bill without giving the Commission on Revenue Allocation time to review and comment on it. This is a procedural constitutional violation — the Commission must be consulted, and Parliament must consider their recommendations.

The Commission on Revenue Allocation reviews a proposed bill on county revenue and recommends changes to better protect small counties. Parliament must table and consider these recommendations before the vote — though Parliament can ultimately decide differently, it cannot pretend the recommendations do not exist.

sections.title: Consolidated Fund and other public funds
sections.simplified: All national government money goes into one central fund — the Consolidated Fund. Nothing can be taken from it without Parliament saying so through an appropriation, or without the Controller of Budget approving the withdrawal. This prevents secret or unauthorised spending.
examples: A ministry begins spending money on an unapproved project by drawing directly from government accounts without a parliamentary appropriation. This is unconstitutional — no money can be withdrawn from the Consolidated Fund without either parliamentary appropriation or a specific constitutional charge.

The Controller of Budget refuses to approve a withdrawal from the Consolidated Fund for a project that lacks proper documentation and authorisation. The Constitution empowers the Controller to do exactly this — financial discipline depends on this gatekeeping function.

sections.title: Revenue Funds for county governments
sections.simplified: Just as there is a Consolidated Fund for national government, each county has its own Revenue Fund. County money cannot be touched without the county assembly's authorisation and the Controller of Budget's approval.
examples: A county governor makes unilateral payments from the county revenue fund without county assembly approval. This is unconstitutional — withdrawals from the county Revenue Fund require either a legal charge or county assembly authorisation.

The Controller of Budget refuses to approve county fund withdrawals because the county has not submitted proper financial reports. The Controller's gatekeeping role applies at county level too — counties cannot bypass this constitutional requirement.

sections.title: Contingencies Fund
sections.simplified: The Contingencies Fund is Kenya's emergency money — for urgent and genuinely unforeseen spending needs that Parliament could not have anticipated in the budget. It is not a slush fund for routine expenses that were simply not budgeted.
examples: A major flood disaster destroys infrastructure in multiple counties. The Cabinet Secretary for Finance can authorise an advance from the Contingencies Fund for emergency response — this is exactly the kind of urgent, unforeseen need it is designed for.

A ministry uses Contingencies Fund money to pay salaries that were simply not included in their budget, claiming it is 'urgent.' Routine expenditures that were foreseeable during budgeting do not qualify — the Fund is for genuinely unforeseen emergencies, not budget failures.

sections.title: Power to impose taxes and charges
sections.simplified: Big national taxes — income tax, VAT, customs — are only for the national government. Counties can tax property and entertainment and charge for services, but they cannot create taxes that disrupt national economic policy or undermine businesses operating across county borders.
examples: A county governor announces that all goods entering the county from other counties will attract a 10% 'county import levy.' This is unconstitutional — counties cannot impose charges that disrupt national economic activities or restrict movement of goods across county boundaries.

A county government levies annual property rates on commercial buildings in the county. This is a constitutionally authorised county tax — property rates are explicitly listed as a county taxing power.

sections.title: Imposition of tax
sections.simplified: Taxes can only be created, changed, or waived by law — no official can invent or remove a tax on their own. And State officers — politicians included — must pay taxes just like everyone else. No government job exempts you from taxation.
examples: A revenue officer waives taxes for a company on his personal decision, with no documentation and no legal authority to do so. This is unconstitutional — tax waivers must be authorised by legislation, properly recorded, and reported to the Auditor-General.

A senior government minister claims her office entitles her to exemption from income tax. No law can exempt State officers from tax based on their office — she must pay income tax just like every other Kenyan.

sections.title: Borrowing by national government
sections.simplified: The national government can borrow money — but Parliament sets the rules and must be informed about the details of any loan. Transparency about government borrowing is constitutionally required, not optional.
examples: Parliament passes a resolution requesting full details of a new government loan from China. The Cabinet Secretary for Finance must present all information — including repayment terms, interest rates, and what the money is for — within 7 days. She cannot delay or refuse.

The government borrows billions from an international institution under terms that Parliament has not approved or been informed of. This violates the constitutional requirement for parliamentary oversight of government borrowing.

sections.title: Borrowing by counties
sections.simplified: Counties cannot borrow money on their own initiative — national government must guarantee the loan and the county assembly must approve it. This prevents counties from accumulating unmanageable debts that they then cannot repay.
examples: A county governor takes out a commercial loan from a bank in the county government's name without county assembly approval or national government guarantee. This is unconstitutional — both conditions must be met before a county can legally borrow.

A county wants to borrow to build a new hospital and goes through the proper process: gets national government agreement to guarantee the loan and tables the borrowing proposal in the county assembly for approval. This is the constitutionally correct procedure.

sections.title: Loan guarantees by national government
sections.simplified: When the national government guarantees a county or parastatal loan — meaning Kenya will repay it if the borrower defaults — Parliament must set the rules for when and how this happens. And an annual public report must disclose all guarantees given.
examples: The national government secretly guarantees a large loan for a parastatal without any parliamentary framework or disclosure. This is unconstitutional — Parliament must prescribe the terms for guarantees and the government must publish an annual report on all guarantees.

Citizens want to know which entities the government has guaranteed loans for and on what terms. The Constitution requires this to be published annually after the financial year — this information is public and citizens can demand access to it.

sections.title: Public debt
sections.simplified: Kenya's national debt — everything borrowed or guaranteed by the national government — is automatically paid from the Consolidated Fund. This means debt repayment comes before other spending; it is a prior charge on national revenue.
examples: Kenya has a loan repayment due to an international bank. Even if Parliament has not passed an appropriation for it, the repayment must still happen — public debt is a charge on the Consolidated Fund that does not require a separate parliamentary vote each time.

The government takes on a new loan that raises total public debt significantly. Citizens and Parliament have the right to be informed — the public debt is a collective obligation of all Kenyans and transparency about its size and terms is constitutionally expected.

sections.title: Commission on Revenue Allocation
sections.simplified: The Commission on Revenue Allocation is the independent body that recommends how national revenue should be shared between the national government and the 47 counties. Its mixed composition — drawing from both houses and having technical expertise — ensures recommendations balance political and technical considerations.
examples: Counties feel the current revenue sharing formula disadvantages them. They can engage the Commission on Revenue Allocation — whose function is precisely to recommend fairer bases for equitable sharing — to review and recommend changes to the formula.

The government tries to change the revenue allocation formula without involving the CRA. This is unconstitutional — the Commission's recommendations must be part of any revenue allocation decision, and its independence is constitutionally protected.

sections.title: Functions of the Commission on Revenue Allocation
sections.simplified: The CRA's main job is to work out a fair formula for sharing money between national government and counties, and between different counties. It also identifies which areas count as marginalised for purposes of the Equalisation Fund.
examples: A county government wants to know why it received a smaller allocation than a neighbouring county. They can engage the CRA to understand the formula — the Commission publishes its criteria and recommendations openly for all county governments.

The CRA determines that a certain arid county qualifies as a 'marginalised area' for Equalisation Fund purposes based on its criteria. This determination enables that county's communities to access targeted additional funds for basic services.

sections.title: Division of revenue
sections.simplified: Every five years, the Senate decides the formula for dividing county revenue among the 47 counties. The National Assembly can review it but needs a two-thirds majority to change it or reject it — giving the Senate strong protection over county revenue allocation.
examples: The Senate is due to pass the five-year revenue allocation resolution. Before doing so, county governors, the public, and the Finance Cabinet Secretary must all be consulted — this is a constitutionally required process, not optional consultation.

The National Assembly wants to change the Senate's revenue allocation resolution by a simple majority vote of 51%. This is insufficient — changing the Senate's resolution requires at least two-thirds of all National Assembly members to vote for the amendment.

sections.title: Annual Division and Allocation of Revenue Bills
sections.simplified: Every year, before the budget cycle ends, the Finance Cabinet Secretary must formally introduce two bills to Parliament — one dividing money between national and county governments, and one dividing the county share among all 47 counties. This is mandatory and must happen on time.
examples: It is March and the Finance CS has not yet introduced the Division of Revenue Bill to Parliament. This is a constitutional violation — the bill must be introduced at least two months before the end of the financial year in June.

A county governor complains that the County Allocation of Revenue Bill was tabled very late, giving counties insufficient time to plan their budgets. The Constitution requires both bills to be introduced with enough lead time — late tabling is unconstitutional.

sections.title: Transfer of equitable share
sections.simplified: When national revenue is allocated to a county, the money must be transferred in full — the national government cannot hold it back, impose conditions on how it is spent, or deduct amounts without constitutional authority. County governments have a right to their full equitable share.
examples: The national government announces it will only release county funds if counties first submit detailed spending plans and get national approval. This is unconstitutional — county allocations must be transferred without conditions.

A county receives its equitable share transfer and notes that the national Treasury deducted a set-off for an old loan without any court order or constitutional provision allowing it. The county can legally challenge that deduction.

sections.title: Form, content and timing of budgets
sections.simplified: Both national and county governments must produce proper annual budgets that are transparent and follow uniform accounting standards. Budgets must show real debt figures and how equity between regions is being maintained — not just optimistic estimates.
examples: A county government produces a budget that fails to show its actual debt obligations or how it plans to fund its programmes. The Constitution requires detailed and transparent budget documents — this county's budget does not meet constitutional standards.

The national budget is presented without any figures on actual or projected national debt. This violates the constitutional requirement that the national budget include a statement on debt — Parliament can demand a corrected budget document.

sections.title: Budget estimates and annual Appropriation Bill
sections.simplified: The Finance Cabinet Secretary must give Parliament the budget estimates at least two months before the financial year ends. A parliamentary committee reviews these and makes recommendations, which Parliament must consider before voting on the budget. This ensures genuine scrutiny rather than rubber-stamping.
examples: The Finance CS submits budget estimates to Parliament only one month before the financial year ends. This violates the two-month minimum required by the Constitution — Parliament needs adequate time to review and make recommendations.

The National Assembly approves the budget without ever considering or debating the committee's recommendations. This is unconstitutional — Parliament must take the committee's findings into account before passing the budget.

sections.title: Expenditure before annual budget is passed
sections.simplified: If Parliament has not passed the budget before the new financial year starts, the government does not shut down. The Controller of Budget can authorise spending of up to 50% of last year's budget to keep essential services running — a constitutionally protected safety valve.
examples: Parliament is still debating the budget when July 1st arrives. The Controller of Budget can authorise government spending of up to half of the previous year's approved budget, so hospitals, schools, and civil servants continue to receive funds while Parliament concludes its debate.

A ministry wants to start a brand-new project not in the previous year's budget before the new Appropriations Act is passed. The Controller of Budget cannot authorise this — only continuation of existing spending up to 50% of last year's level is permitted.

sections.title: Supplementary appropriation
sections.simplified: Governments can spend unbudgeted money in emergencies or for unforeseen needs — but only up to 10% of the total approved budget, and only if the legislature formally approves it within two months. This prevents unlimited emergency spending without accountability.
examples: After floods destroy bridges, a county government spends money from unappropriated funds to fund emergency repairs. This is lawful if the amount is within 10% of the approved budget and the county assembly ratifies the expenditure within two months.

A national ministry wants to spend money that exceeds 10% of its approved budget under a supplementary appropriation claim. This is unconstitutional — the 10% cap is a hard limit that cannot be exceeded without a new Appropriations Act.

sections.title: County appropriation Bills
sections.simplified: County governments cannot spend any money from their county revenue fund without first getting county assembly approval. The elected representatives of the county — not the governor or executives alone — must authorise all spending.
examples: A county governor instructs the county treasury to pay a contractor from the county revenue fund before the county assembly has approved the expenditure. This is unconstitutional — no money may be spent without prior county assembly appropriation.

A county assembly passes its appropriation bill before the financial year begins, enabling the county government to legally start spending on approved programmes from the first day of the new year. This is the constitutionally correct process.

sections.title: Financial control
sections.simplified: Parliament must create laws governing how public money is managed. If a county government repeatedly and seriously mismanages its finances, the national government's Finance Cabinet Secretary has the power to step in — but only through a formal, legally prescribed process.
examples: A county government repeatedly fails to submit audited accounts, fails to pay civil servants, and accumulates unsustainable debt. After following the constitutionally prescribed process, the Finance Cabinet Secretary can intervene in the county's financial management.

The Finance Cabinet Secretary tries to take over a county's budget because she disagrees with the governor's spending priorities. This is not a valid basis for intervention — only persistent and material failure to meet financial management obligations justifies intervention.

sections.title: Accounts and audit of public entities
sections.simplified: Every public institution — national ministries, county governments, parastatals — must have their accounts audited by the independent Auditor-General. And the accounting officer — usually a Principal Secretary or equivalent — is personally accountable to Parliament or the county assembly for how money was managed.
examples: A county CEO refuses to allow the Auditor-General access to the county enterprise's financial records, claiming they are a private company. If public funds are involved, the Auditor-General has a constitutional right to audit those accounts.

The Auditor-General tables a report showing serious financial irregularities in a national ministry. The Principal Secretary of that ministry must appear before the relevant parliamentary committee and answer for those irregularities — they are personally accountable.

sections.title: Procurement of public goods and services
sections.simplified: Government contracts must be awarded through a process that is fair, open, competitive, and gives value for taxpayers' money. No ministry or county can just pick any supplier it wants — procurement must follow rules that are designed to prevent corruption and favouritism.
examples: A county government awards a road construction contract to the governor's brother without any competitive tendering process. This violates the constitutional requirement for fair, transparent, and competitive procurement — it can be challenged in court.

A ministry invites bids for a project but sets qualification criteria so specific that only one company can qualify, effectively eliminating competition. This undermines the constitutional procurement principles of fairness and competitiveness.

sections.title: Controller of Budget
sections.simplified: The Controller of Budget is the independent official who ensures that money is only withdrawn from government accounts when it has been properly approved, and who tracks how both national and county budgets are actually being spent. Their quarterly reports give Parliament the power to catch problems early.
examples: The Controller of Budget's quarterly report reveals that a county government has spent 90% of its annual development budget in just the first four months of the year — mostly in suspicious payments. Parliament and the public can use this report to demand accountability.

A ministry tries to make a large withdrawal from the Consolidated Fund without proper appropriation documentation. The Controller of Budget must refuse to authorise the withdrawal — their oversight role is to prevent unauthorised withdrawals.

sections.title: Auditor-General
sections.simplified: The Auditor-General is Kenya's independent financial watchdog. They serve one non-renewable eight-year term — protecting them from political pressure — and must audit all public accounts and report to Parliament within six months of each financial year ending.
examples: The financial year ends in June and by October the Auditor-General has not yet tabled audit reports to Parliament. The Constitution requires the reports within six months — Parliament can formally demand them and the Auditor-General can be held accountable for the delay.

A new President tries to fire the Auditor-General for publishing a report exposing financial mismanagement in his administration. The Auditor-General's appointment process and single fixed term are designed precisely to prevent this kind of political interference.

sections.title: Salaries and Remuneration Commission
sections.simplified: The Salaries and Remuneration Commission independently sets and reviews the pay of all State officers — including the President, MPs, governors, and judges. No politician can set their own salary or allow Parliament to vote itself a pay rise — an independent commission does it.
examples: Members of Parliament push through a resolution doubling their own salaries. They cannot lawfully do this — the SRC has the exclusive constitutional mandate to set State officer remuneration. The resolution has no legal force.

A county governor complains that her salary has not kept up with inflation. She can formally petition the SRC to review county executive remuneration — this is the constitutionally correct process for adjusting State officer pay.

sections.title: Central Bank of Kenya
sections.simplified: The Central Bank of Kenya is constitutionally independent. It controls monetary policy and currency — and no politician, President, or minister can direct it on how to exercise those functions. This independence protects Kenya's economy from political manipulation.
examples: The President's office issues a directive to the Central Bank to lower interest rates before an election to boost short-term economic activity. The Central Bank is constitutionally protected from such direction and can lawfully refuse to comply.

Parliament passes a law requiring the Central Bank to print money to fund government spending. The Central Bank's constitutional mandate of price stability and independence means it must resist directives that undermine monetary stability.

chapters.title: THE PUBLIC SERVICE
chapters.description: Rules for public officers and service delivery

sections.title: Values and principles of public service
sections.simplified: Public servants — from hospital nurses to immigration officers to county clerks — must serve all Kenyans with professionalism, speed, fairness, and accountability. Representing Kenya's diversity is not just a policy goal, it is a constitutional value binding on all public institutions.
examples: A government department consistently takes months to respond to citizen applications and when pressed, gives no explanation. This violates the constitutional public service values of responsiveness, promptness, and provision of accurate information to the public.

A national institution's senior staff are all drawn from one ethnic community. The Constitution requires that public service reflect Kenya's diverse communities — this lack of diversity is a constitutional concern that citizens and oversight bodies can raise.

sections.title: The Public Service Commission
sections.simplified: The PSC is the independent body responsible for hiring, managing, and disciplining civil servants in the national government. It creates and abolishes government positions — ensuring that public service appointments are based on merit rather than political patronage.
examples: A minister tries to directly hire a friend into a senior civil service role, bypassing the Public Service Commission. The PSC has constitutional authority over such appointments — the minister cannot make the appointment unilaterally.

The national government wants to create a new department to coordinate climate change programmes. The Public Service Commission must formally establish the office and define its structure — new government offices cannot just be created informally.

sections.title: Functions and powers of the Public Service Commission
sections.simplified: The PSC does far more than just hire people — it also manages the entire lifecycle of civil service employment, investigates violations of public service standards, and actively promotes the constitutional values that all public servants must uphold.
examples: A citizen complains that a government official demanded a bribe before processing a service. The PSC has the power to investigate violations of public service values and can take disciplinary action against that officer.

A public servant is promoted over more qualified colleagues because of their ethnic background. The PSC is constitutionally mandated to handle promotions based on merit and to investigate breaches of the fairness and non-discrimination values.

sections.title: Staffing of county governments
sections.simplified: Each of the 47 counties has its own public service and its own public service board. County governments manage their own civil servants — hiring, promoting, and disciplining them — but must follow the same constitutional values of merit, fairness, and diversity that apply nationally.
examples: A county governor dismisses a county health officer who exposed procurement irregularities in his administration. The county public service board — not the governor — handles discipline, and such a dismissal would need to follow proper procedures and cannot be used to victimise whistleblowers.

A county public service board hires all new county officers from only one sub-county, ignoring candidates from other parts of the county. This violates the constitutional requirement that the public service reflect the diversity of the communities it serves.

sections.title: Protection of public officers
sections.simplified: Public servants have a constitutional right to join trade unions and to participate in lawful union activities without fear of punishment. A government employer cannot fire, demote, or victimise a civil servant just because they are a union member or went on a lawful strike.
examples: A hospital matron is demoted after she helped organise health workers to formally petition management about poor working conditions through their union. This demotion is unconstitutional — participation in legitimate trade union activities is protected.

A government ministry threatens to dismiss any employee who attends a trade union meeting on their day off. The Constitution prohibits victimisation of public officers for union membership — this threat is unlawful.

sections.title: Teachers Service Commission
sections.simplified: The national government has a constitutional duty to actively bring Kenyans together across ethnic and racial lines. And in running public affairs, no person can be discriminated against because of their ethnicity or race — this applies to hiring, service delivery, and every aspect of how government operates.
examples: A government department systematically rejects job applicants from certain regions of Kenya, favouring candidates from the minister's home county. This is direct ethnic discrimination in public affairs — unconstitutional and legally challengeable.

The government launches programmes specifically aimed at fostering shared national identity and reducing ethnic tensions during a period of heightened community conflict. This is precisely what the constitutional duty of national cohesion requires.

chapters.title: NATIONAL SECURITY
chapters.description: How the country is kept secure

sections.title: Principles of national security
sections.simplified: Security forces — the military, police, intelligence services — do not have unlimited power in the name of security. Everything they do must comply with the Constitution, respect human rights, and be subject to democratic oversight. Security cannot be used as an excuse to violate rights.
examples: Intelligence officers conduct warrantless surveillance of citizens' communications, claiming national security justifies it. This is unconstitutional — national security operations must comply with the law and respect fundamental freedoms including privacy.

A security agency detains a journalist for two weeks without charge, citing national security concerns. This violates the Constitution — even security operations must respect human rights and the rule of law, including the right not to be detained without charge.

sections.title: National security organs
sections.simplified: Kenya has three constitutionally established security bodies, each with a distinct purpose — the military defends the country, intelligence detects threats, and the police maintain order. Each body has defined boundaries and cannot lawfully act outside its constitutional mandate.
examples: The military is deployed to break up a peaceful public demonstration. Unless there is a legal declaration of a state of emergency, deploying the KDF for routine internal policing is outside their constitutional mandate — that is the role of the National Police Service.

The National Intelligence Service gathers evidence that an official is involved in corruption and arrests him using police powers. The NIS has no arrest powers — it detects threats and passes intelligence to appropriate bodies. Arrests are the mandate of the police.

sections.title: Establishment of the National Security Council
sections.simplified: The National Security Council is the senior constitutional body that oversees all of Kenya's security organs. By including civilian cabinet members and the Attorney-General, the Constitution ensures that civilian and legal oversight is built into security decision-making at the highest level.
examples: Kenya faces a serious cross-border security threat involving both the military and intelligence services. The National Security Council — bringing together the President, key Cabinet Secretaries, and the Attorney-General — is the constitutionally designated body to coordinate the national response.

The NSC is convened to review intelligence reports on domestic terrorism. The Attorney-General's presence ensures that any security operations recommended are checked against legal and constitutional requirements before being authorised.

sections.title: Establishment of Kenya Defence Forces and Defence Council
sections.simplified: The KDF exists to defend Kenya — not to police citizens. The President commands the military but cannot unilaterally send troops outside Kenya without Parliament's approval. This ensures that decisions to go to war or join foreign missions involve democratic accountability.
examples: Kenya is asked to contribute troops to an African Union peacekeeping mission in another country. Even if the President agrees, Parliament must approve the deployment of the KDF outside Kenyan territory before troops can lawfully be sent.

A county governor requests military assistance to manage post-election violence. The KDF can only be deployed internally under specific constitutional conditions — routine internal security is the mandate of the National Police Service.

sections.title: Establishment of National Intelligence Service
sections.simplified: The NIS is Kenya's intelligence agency — it gathers and analyses information about threats to national security. Crucially, it has no powers of arrest and cannot investigate ordinary criminal matters. It collects intelligence; it does not act as a police force or a secret police.
examples: NIS officers detain a businessman at the airport and hold him for three days for questioning about his business associates. The NIS has no powers of arrest — only the police can lawfully detain someone, and this detention is unconstitutional.

The NIS receives credible intelligence about a planned terrorist attack. Its constitutional role is to collect that intelligence and share it with the appropriate security organs — the police or KDF — who then have the authority to act on it.

sections.title: Establishment of the National Police Service
sections.simplified: The National Police Service is the principal law enforcement body in Kenya, with the power to arrest, investigate crime, and maintain order. It is a single national service — there is no separate county police — though counties coordinate closely with the police.
examples: A county government decides to create its own county police force separate from the National Police Service to patrol county offices. This is unconstitutional — policing is a national function, and there is only one constitutionally established police service.

A person witnesses a crime and calls the police for assistance. The National Police Service is constitutionally mandated to respond, investigate, and protect that person — this is not a favour, it is their constitutional duty.

sections.title: Objects and functions of the National Police Service
sections.simplified: The police must be professional, politically neutral, and respectful of the people they serve. They are not a tool of any political party or government — they serve all Kenyans equally. And crucially, they answer to civilian oversight — not only to their commanders.
examples: Police officers are seen at political rallies in uniform, assisting in protecting only one party's gatherings while ignoring requests from opposition supporters. This partisan conduct violates the constitutional requirement that the police be non-partisan and serve all citizens equally.

A police officer brutally beats a suspect during arrest. This violates the Police Service's constitutional duty to respect human rights and dignity — the victim can sue the State and the officer can face disciplinary and criminal consequences.

sections.title: Command of the National Police Service
sections.simplified: The Inspector-General runs day-to-day police operations independently. The Cabinet Secretary can set policy direction but cannot tell the IG how to handle any specific investigation. This protects police investigations from political interference.
examples: The Cabinet Secretary for Internal Security calls the Inspector-General and instructs him to drop an investigation into a politically connected official. The Cabinet Secretary has no constitutional authority to direct the IG on specific investigations — the IG should refuse.

The Cabinet Secretary issues a policy circular requiring all county commands to improve their crime reporting systems. This is a legitimate policy direction — the Cabinet Secretary can set policy but cannot interfere with specific operational or investigative decisions.

sections.title: National Police Service Commission
sections.simplified: The National Police Service Commission is the oversight body that hires, manages, and disciplines police officers — independently of political control. It is designed to prevent police appointments and discipline from being driven by political connections rather than merit.
examples: A police officer is dismissed from the service following a criminal conviction. The National Police Service Commission handles such disciplinary processes — the dismissal must follow proper procedures and the officer has the right to contest it through the Commission.

A politician pressures the Inspector-General to promote a politically connected officer above more qualified candidates. The NPSC's independent role in managing promotions is designed precisely to resist this kind of interference.

sections.title: Other police services
sections.simplified: Parliament can create additional security bodies beyond the KDF, NIS, and Police — but any such bodies must adhere to constitutional values of accountability, non-discrimination, and human rights. Security bodies cannot be created as tools of oppression.
examples: Parliament creates a specialised cybersecurity agency to handle digital threats to national infrastructure. This is within Parliament's constitutional authority — but the agency must still be bound by constitutional values of accountability and human rights.

Parliament passes a law creating a parallel security agency that operates outside any oversight framework. Even though Parliament authorised it, such an agency would violate the constitutional principles that all security organs must comply with.

chapters.title: COMMISSIONS AND INDEPENDENT OFFICES
chapters.fullTitle: Chapter Fifteen – COMMISSIONS AND INDEPENDENT OFFICES

sections.title: Application of Chapter
sections.simplified: Chapter 15 establishes a group of independent constitutional commissions and offices that are deliberately kept free from government control. They serve as watchdogs, enforcers of rights, and guardians of integrity — and this chapter sets out the rules that apply to all of them.
examples: A citizen wants to report human rights violations by government officials. The Kenya National Human Rights Commission is one of the independent constitutional bodies created specifically to receive and investigate such complaints — it is independent of the government being complained about.

The Ethics and Anti-Corruption Commission investigates a corrupt senior official. Because it is a constitutional commission, the government cannot lawfully interfere with or shut down the investigation — its independence is constitutionally guaranteed.

sections.title: Objects, authority and funding of commissions and independent offices
sections.simplified: Constitutional commissions exist to protect ordinary citizens from abuses of power and to make sure Kenya's democratic principles are actually practised. Their funding directly from the Consolidated Fund means the government cannot starve them of resources to silence them.
examples: A government ministry tries to freeze funding to the Ethics and Anti-Corruption Commission after it opens a probe into the ministry. This is unconstitutional — commissions are funded directly from the Consolidated Fund and cannot be defunded by the executive.

A marginalised pastoral community has its concerns consistently ignored by both the national and county governments. The Kenya National Human Rights Commission is constitutionally mandated to give voice to exactly such marginalised groups and can formally investigate their complaints.

sections.title: Composition, appointment and terms of office
sections.simplified: Commission appointments must represent all of Kenya — not just Nairobi or any one community. The process must be open and competitive, and gender balance is not optional. This prevents commissions from being captured by any single ethnic, regional, or gender group.
examples: The President appoints six new members of a constitutional commission but all are from his own ethnic community. This violates the constitutional requirement that appointments reflect Kenya's regional and ethnic diversity — the appointments can be legally challenged.

A commission recruitment process is conducted behind closed doors with no public advertisement or participation. The Constitution requires a transparent, competitive process involving public participation — this secretive process is unconstitutional.

sections.title: Removal from office
sections.simplified: You cannot remove a commission member just because you disagree with them or they are inconvenient to those in power. Removal requires serious, specific grounds and a formal process involving a tribunal, presidential action, and Parliament — not a unilateral executive decision.
examples: The President is unhappy with rulings made by the chairperson of the IEBC and attempts to dismiss her by executive order. This is unconstitutional — removal requires a formal tribunal process and parliamentary approval. The chairperson can go to court to challenge the illegal dismissal.

A commission member is shown to have been taking bribes from parties they were supposed to regulate. Gross misconduct of this severity is one of the constitutionally specified grounds for removal — and the formal removal process can now be triggered.

sections.title: General functions and powers
sections.simplified: Constitutional commissions are not just advisory bodies — they have teeth. They can summon people, demand documents, compel state organs to assist them, take action to remedy wrongs, and push for law or policy changes. Their findings are not easily ignored.
examples: The Commission on Administrative Justice investigates a complaint that a government ministry unreasonably denied a citizen's access to information. The Commission can summon ministry officials, demand records, and require the ministry to reverse its decision.

After investigating a series of election-related abuses, the IEBC recommends new legislation to Parliament to close legal loopholes. This is within the Commission's constitutional mandate to recommend legal reform in its area of work.

sections.title: Incorporation of commissions and independent offices
sections.simplified: This is one of the most important protections in the Constitution — commissions cannot be told what to do or not do by the President, Cabinet, Parliament, or any other authority. Their independence is absolute when exercising their constitutional functions.
examples: The Attorney-General instructs the Kenya National Human Rights Commission not to publish a report critical of the government's treatment of protesters. The KNCHR is constitutionally independent and the Attorney-General has no authority to suppress its reports.

A Cabinet Secretary calls the EACC to tell them to prioritise certain investigations and shelve others. The EACC cannot lawfully comply with such direction — it independently determines its own investigative priorities.

sections.title: Reporting by commissions and independent offices
sections.simplified: Commissions must report annually to both the President and Parliament — and must also make those reports available to the public. This dual accountability — to the executive and the legislature — and the public disclosure requirement ensure that commissions cannot operate in secrecy.
examples: The National Cohesion and Integration Commission has not published its annual report for three consecutive years. This is a constitutional violation — the Commission is required to report annually and to publish those reports so that the public can hold it accountable.

Parliament schedules a session specifically to review the annual reports of constitutional commissions. This oversight role — enabled by the constitutional reporting requirement — allows legislators to question commission leadership about their performance and priorities.

chapters.title: AMENDMENT OF THIS CONSTITUTION
chapters.fullTitle: Chapter Sixteen – AMENDMENT OF THIS CONSTITUTION
chapters.description: How the Constitution can be changed

sections.title: Amendment of this Constitution
sections.simplified: Certain parts of the Constitution are so fundamental that Parliament alone cannot change them — Kenyans must vote in a referendum. Term limits for the President, the Bill of Rights, devolution, and the structure of government cannot be altered without the people's direct consent.
examples: Parliament passes a bill extending the presidential term limit from two to three terms. Because presidential term limits are a constitutionally protected matter requiring a referendum, the bill has no force until it is approved by voters in a national referendum.

A constitutional amendment is proposed to reduce the powers of county governments. Since the system of devolution is a referendum matter, this cannot be changed by Parliament alone — Kenyans must directly vote on it.

sections.title: Amendment by parliamentary initiative
sections.simplified: Not every constitutional change requires a referendum — for matters not listed in Article 255, Parliament can amend the Constitution on its own, but only with approval from both the National Assembly and the Senate. And if the change affects devolution, county assemblies must also approve it.
examples: Parliament proposes a constitutional amendment to change the composition of a parliamentary committee structure. This is not a referendum matter — it can proceed through both Houses, but both must approve it before it becomes law.

An amendment is proposed that changes how county assemblies operate. Even if it is not a referendum matter, the majority of county assemblies must formally approve the change — they cannot have their powers altered without their own consent.

sections.title: Amendment by popular initiative
sections.simplified: Ordinary Kenyans — not just politicians — can propose constitutional amendments. If you collect one million registered voter signatures, you can start a constitutional reform process. This is a direct democratic tool to change the Constitution without going through politicians.
examples: A civic group organises a popular initiative to amend the Constitution to make femicide a constitutionally recognised concern. After collecting one million signatures, the proposal must go through county assemblies and both houses of Parliament before it can become a constitutional amendment.

A million Kenyans sign a popular initiative proposing to reduce presidential term limits to one term. Because term limits are a referendum matter under Article 255, even if Parliament supports it, the change must ultimately go to a national referendum for voters to decide.

sections.title: Enforcement of this Constitution
sections.simplified: Any Kenyan — not just lawyers or politicians — can go to court if they believe the Constitution has been broken or is about to be broken. You do not need to prove that you personally were harmed. The Constitution belongs to everyone and anyone can defend it.
examples: A student notices that Parliament is about to pass a law that conflicts with the Constitution. She can personally file a court petition — she does not need to be a lawyer or a direct victim of the law — because every Kenyan has the right to protect the Constitution.

A retired civil servant believes the government is following an unconstitutional process to amend the Constitution. He has the constitutional right to go to the High Court and challenge the process, even though he is not personally affected by the specific amendment being proposed.

sections.title: Construing this Constitution
sections.simplified: When judges or officials interpret the Constitution, they must always choose the reading that best promotes the Constitution's purposes — especially human dignity, equality, and democracy. If there is any doubt about how much a right can be limited, the interpretation that limits it least wins.
examples: A court is interpreting an ambiguous constitutional provision about freedom of assembly. Two interpretations are possible — one that allows broad restrictions and one that allows minimal restrictions. The Constitution requires the judge to prefer the interpretation that limits the right least.

The Attorney-General interprets a constitutional clause in a way that reduces citizens' rights to access government information. If a broader, rights-affirming interpretation is equally valid, courts must prefer that interpretation — the Constitution must be read in a way that best promotes its values.

sections.title: Interpretation
sections.simplified: This article defines key terms used throughout the Constitution to ensure consistent meaning. Knowing these definitions matters — for example, 'Parliament' means both Houses together, not just the National Assembly, and 'disability' is defined very broadly to include mental and psychological conditions, not only physical ones.
examples: A law says 'Parliament must approve this' — a minister argues the National Assembly alone can act. This is wrong — the Constitution defines Parliament as both the National Assembly and the Senate, so both houses must be involved.

A person with a psychological condition claims they are covered by constitutional disability protections. The Constitution defines disability very broadly — including psychological impairments — so they are entitled to the same protection as someone with a physical disability.

chapters.title: TRANSITIONAL AND CONSEQUENTIAL PROVISIONS
chapters.fullTitle: Chapter Eighteen – TRANSITIONAL AND CONSEQUENTIAL PROVISIONS

sections.title: Consequential legislation
sections.simplified: When the Constitution says Parliament shall enact a law, Parliament must actually do it — and within the timelines set. If Parliament drags its feet, any Kenyan can go to court to force Parliament to act. Constitutional obligations on Parliament are not optional.
examples: The Constitution requires Parliament to enact legislation on a specific subject within two years but five years have passed and no law has been passed. A citizen can petition the High Court to compel Parliament to enact the required legislation.

Parliament passes many of the required laws but leaves out legislation on a specific constitutional right. The persons whose rights are affected can go to court — Parliament's constitutional duty to legislate is enforceable by any citizen.

sections.title: Transitional and consequential provisions
sections.simplified: When the 2010 Constitution came into force, it had to work alongside existing laws, institutions, and officeholders that were created under the old constitution. This article allows the transitional schedule to manage that handover — so the change from the old to the new constitutional order was orderly.
examples: When the 2010 Constitution came into force, sitting judges were not automatically dismissed — the transitional provisions set out a process for reviewing the continuation of their appointments. This orderly transition was enabled by the transitional schedule.

Laws that were inconsistent with the new Constitution were not all immediately void — the transitional schedule gave Parliament deadlines to amend or replace them. This prevented chaos and gave institutions time to align with the new constitutional order.

sections.title: Effective Date
sections.simplified: The 2010 Constitution came into force when it was formally promulgated — signed into law by the President — on 27 August 2010, following its approval in the August 2010 referendum. This article establishes the legal mechanism that gave the Constitution its force of law.
examples: After Kenyans voted overwhelmingly for the new Constitution in the August 2010 referendum, President Kibaki promulgated it at Uhuru Park on 27 August 2010. That act of promulgation gave the Constitution its legal force under this article.

Had the President not promulgated the Constitution within 14 days of the referendum results being announced, the Constitution would have automatically come into force by expiry of the 14-day period — the President could not block it by simply refusing to sign.

sections.title: Repeal of previous Constitution
sections.simplified: When the 2010 Constitution came into force, it completely replaced the old 1963 Independence Constitution (as amended over the years). There is no hybrid or partial retention — the old constitution was fully repealed and the 2010 Constitution stands alone as the supreme law.
examples: After August 27, 2010, no provision of the old Kenyan constitution has any legal force. If anyone tries to rely on a provision from the old constitution to justify an action or right, they are relying on repealed law — it is the 2010 Constitution that now governs.

Lawyers and judges who were trained under the old constitution had to transition to interpreting and applying the 2010 Constitution from the date of its promulgation. The old constitution ceased to exist as a legal document on that date.
